verses.flow: Paul, a called apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, and Sosthenes the brother,
verses.note: GREEK: "Klētos" (κλητός, G2822) means "called" or "summoned" — not self-appointed but divinely commissioned. "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, G652) denotes one sent with authority, a commissioned envoy. Paul establishes his authority immediately as deriving from the will (θέλημα) of God, not from human institution. HISTORICAL: Sosthenes may be the synagogue ruler mentioned in Acts 18:17 who was beaten before Gallio's tribunal in Corinth, possibly later converted.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative singular feminine
verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: To the assembly of God that is in Corinth, having been sanctified in Christ Jesus, called holy ones, together with all those calling upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ in every place, both theirs and ours:
verses.note: GREEK: "Ekklēsia" (ἐκκλησία, G1577) derives from "ek" (out of) + "kaleō" (to call) — the "called-out assembly." In the LXX this term renders Hebrew "qahal," the congregation of Israel. "Hēgiasmenois" (ἡγιασμένοις) is a perfect passive participle — sanctification is a completed divine action with ongoing results. THEOLOGICAL: The scope broadens from local Corinth to "all those calling upon the name" everywhere, establishing the universal lordship of Christ and the unity of his people across all locations.

verses.flow: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Grace and peace" (charis kai eirēnē) combines the Greek greeting (chairein) with the Hebrew blessing (shalom). Paul theologizes both by grounding them in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, placed in coordinate relationship — a striking early Christological declaration. The formula echoes the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:24-26.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: I give thanks to my God always concerning you, for the grace of God that was given to you in Christ Jesus,
that in everything you were enriched in him, in all speech and all knowledge,
even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: so that you lack in no gift, eagerly awaiting the revelation of our Lord Jesus Christ,
who also will confirm you unto the end, blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
Faithful is God, through whom you were called into the fellowship of his Son, Jesus Christ our Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pistos ho theos" — "Faithful is God" — places the predicate adjective first for emphasis. The faithfulness of God is the ground of the believer's perseverance, not human effort. "Koinōnia" (κοινωνία, G2842) means deep fellowship, communion, and participation — believers are called into shared life with the Son himself. This verse anchors the entire exhortation against division that follows.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Now I exhort you, brothers, through the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that you all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you, but that you be made complete in the same mind and in the same judgment.
verses.note: GREEK: "Schismata" (σχίσματα, G4978) literally means "tears" or "rips" — as in torn cloth (cf. Matt 9:16). "Katērtismenoi" (κατηρτισμένοι, G2675) is used of mending nets (Matt 4:21) and setting broken bones in medical literature — Paul envisions the restoration of a fractured community. STRUCTURE: This verse introduces the first major section of the letter (1:10–4:21) addressing factionalism in the Corinthian assembly.

verses.flow: For it was made known to me concerning you, my brothers, by those of Chloe, that there are contentions among you.
Now I say this, that each of you says, "I am of Paul," and "I am of Apollos," and "I am of Cephas," and "I am of Christ."
Has Christ been divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized into the name of Paul?
I give thanks that I baptized none of you except Crispus and Gaius,
so that no one might say that you were baptized into my name.
Now I baptized also the household of Stephanas; beyond that, I do not know whether I baptized any other.
For Christ did not send me to baptize, but to proclaim the good news — not in wisdom of speech, lest the cross of Christ be made void.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes his apostolic commission — proclamation (euangelizesthai) not ritual administration (baptizein). "Ouk en sophia logou" — "not in wisdom of speech" — sets up the great contrast between human rhetoric and divine power that dominates 1:18–2:16. "Kenoō" (κενόω, G2758) means to empty or make void; eloquent human wisdom would drain the cross of its inherent saving power.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine
verb present passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: For the word of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ho logos ho tou staurou" — "the word of the cross" — is the theological center of this chapter. The present participles "apollymenois" (perishing) and "sōzomenois" (being saved) indicate ongoing states — humanity divides into two groups based on response to the cross. The cross as "dynamis theou" (power of God) is a paradox: the instrument of Rome's shame becomes the vehicle of divine omnipotence.

verses.flow: For it has been written: "I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the understanding of the discerning I will set aside."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Quoted from Isaiah 29:14 (LXX). The original context is God's judgment on Jerusalem's leaders who relied on political alliances with Egypt rather than trusting God. Paul applies this to the failure of all human wisdom systems — Jewish, Greek, or otherwise — to achieve what only God's "foolish" cross-work accomplishes. "Athetēsō" (ἀθετήσω, G114) means to annul or set aside as invalid.

verses.flow: Where is the wise one? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?
verses.note: GREEK: "Syzētētēs" (συζητητής, G4804) is a NT hapax legomenon — a "co-debater" or "disputer," likely referring to Greco-Roman philosophical disputants. HISTORICAL: Paul's triple question echoes Isaiah 33:18 (LXX) and evokes the three pillars of ancient intellectual culture: the Jewish sage (sophos), the Torah scholar (grammateus), and the Greek philosopher-debater (syzētētēs). DIVINE_COUNCIL: God as sovereign over all wisdom traditions — he "made foolish" (emōranen) the world's wisdom, demonstrating that no created intellect can penetrate divine counsel apart from revelation.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For since, in the wisdom of God, the world through its wisdom did not know God, God was well pleased through the foolishness of the proclamation to save those who believe,
since indeed Jews ask for signs and Greeks seek wisdom,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: but we proclaim Christ crucified — to Jews indeed a stumbling block, and to Gentiles foolishness,
verses.note: GREEK: "Skandalon" (σκάνδαλον, G4625) originally denoted the trigger-stick of a trap — hence "stumbling block" or "snare." THEOLOGICAL: A crucified Messiah was a contradiction in terms for Second Temple Jewish expectation, which anticipated a conquering Davidic king. For Greeks, the idea that ultimate truth and divine power could be manifested in a criminal's execution was absurd. Yet this "scandal" is precisely the means of God's saving action.

verses.flow: but to those who are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.
For the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than men.
For consider your calling, brothers, that not many were wise according to the flesh, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth.
But the foolish things of the world God chose, that he might put to shame the wise; and the weak things of the world God chose, that he might put to shame the strong;

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural neuter
verb present active participle accusative plural neuter
verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: and the lowborn things of the world and the things that are despised God chose — the things that are not — that he might bring to nothing the things that are,
verses.note: GREEK: "Agenē" (ἀγενῆ, G36) means "of no family" or "lowborn" — the opposite of "eugenēs" (noble) in v.26. "Ta mē onta" — "the things that are not" — echoes the language of creation ex nihilo and may allude to God calling things into existence from nothing (Rom 4:17). DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's sovereign election overturns every earthly hierarchy and power structure, demonstrating that his purposes operate according to a wholly different principle than human honor-shame systems.

verses.flow: so that no flesh might boast before God.
But from him you are in Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God, both righteousness and sanctification and redemption,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Christ is identified as wisdom (sophia) from God — a direct counter to the Corinthians' pursuit of human wisdom. The fourfold description — wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption — may trace the order of salvation: Christ as the true wisdom reveals God's righteousness, effects our sanctification, and accomplishes our final redemption. TYPOLOGY: Christ as divine Wisdom personified echoes Proverbs 8 and Wisdom of Solomon 7, fulfilling the role attributed to God's Wisdom in creation and salvation.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: so that, even as it has been written, "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Quoted from Jeremiah 9:24 (LXX 9:23). In the original context, Jeremiah contrasts boasting in wisdom, might, and riches with boasting in knowing the Lord — precisely the three categories Paul has been dismantling throughout this chapter. "En kyriō" — "in the Lord" — when read through Paul's Christological lens, the Lord in whom one boasts is Jesus Christ himself, the wisdom and power of God (v.24).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I, having come to you, brothers, came not according to excellence of speech or of wisdom, proclaiming to you the testimony of God.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I determined not to know anything among you except Jesus Christ, and Him having been crucified.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἔκρινα" (ekrina, from krinō, Strong's G2919) here means "determined" or "resolved," not "judged." The perfect passive participle "ἐσταυρωμένον" (estaurōmenon) emphasizes the abiding state of Christ as crucified — His crucifixion is not merely a past event but a present reality defining the gospel message. THEOLOGICAL: Paul deliberately narrows his entire theological program to the cross, countering Corinthian fascination with rhetorical sophistication.

verses.flow: And I, in weakness and in fear and in much trembling, came to be among you.
And my speech and my proclamation were not in persuasive words of wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power,
so that your faith might not be in the wisdom of men but in the power of God.

words.m: verb present passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Yet wisdom we do speak among the mature — but a wisdom not of this age, nor of the rulers of this age, who are being brought to nothing;
verses.note: GREEK: "τελείοις" (teleiois, Strong's G5046) means "mature" or "complete," not "perfect" in a moral sense; it contrasts with the "infants in Christ" of 3:1. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "ἀρχόντων τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου" (archontōn tou aiōnos toutou) — "rulers of this age" — carries a dual referent: both human authorities (Pilate, Caiaphas) and the spiritual powers behind them (cf. Eph 6:12; Col 2:15). The participle "καταργουμένων" (katargoumenōn, present passive) indicates these rulers are presently being rendered powerless.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: but we speak the wisdom of God in a mystery, the hidden wisdom which God foreordained before the ages unto our glory,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ἐν μυστηρίῳ" (en mystēriō) — "in a mystery" — does not mean "mysterious" but rather a divine secret now being revealed in the gospel age. "προώρισεν" (proōrisen, Strong's G4309) — "foreordained" — places God's redemptive wisdom before creation itself (cf. Eph 1:4-5, 2 Tim 1:9). TYPOLOGY: The hidden wisdom foreordained "before the ages" echoes the eternal decree of God's salvific plan, fulfilled in Christ's crucifixion and glorification.

verses.flow: which none of the rulers of this age has known; for if they had known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "τῶν ἀρχόντων" (tōn archontōn) functions at both the earthly and heavenly level. The crucifixion was orchestrated by human rulers acting under the influence of cosmic powers who, ironically, accomplished their own defeat. THEOLOGICAL: "τὸν κύριον τῆς δόξης" (ton kyrion tēs doxēs) — "the Lord of glory" — is a supreme Christological title linking Jesus to the divine kavod/doxa of YHWH (cf. Ps 24:7-10 LXX; Jas 2:1). The rulers' ignorance of God's hidden wisdom led them to crucify the very divine Lord.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: But as it has been written: "Things which eye has not seen and ear has not heard, and which have not entered into the heart of man — all that God has prepared for those who love Him."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation draws from Isaiah 64:4 and Isaiah 65:17 (LXX), with possible echoes of Jeremiah 3:16. Paul adapts the LXX text freely to emphasize the radical newness of God's eschatological revelation. THEOLOGICAL: The triad of "eye," "ear," and "heart" represents the totality of human perception — none of which can access divine wisdom apart from revelation.

verses.flow: For to us God revealed them through the Spirit; for the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἀπεκάλυψεν" (apekalypsen, Strong's G601) — "revealed" or "unveiled" — is an apocalyptic term indicating divine disclosure of what was previously hidden. "ἐραυνᾷ" (eraunai, Strong's G2045) means "searches" or "investigates thoroughly." THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit's role as the one who searches "τὰ βάθη τοῦ θεοῦ" (ta bathē tou theou) — "the deep things of God" — establishes the Spirit's full divinity and intimate access to the inner life of God Himself.

verses.flow: For who among men knows the things of a man except the spirit of the man which is in him? So also the things of God no one has known except the Spirit of God.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: Now we received not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit which is from God, so that we might know the things freely given to us by God,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: which things also we speak, not in words taught by human wisdom, but in words taught by the Spirit, interpreting spiritual things by spiritual means.
But the natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him, and he is not able to know them, because they are spiritually discerned.
verses.note: GREEK: "ψυχικός" (psychikos, Strong's G5591) — "natural" or "soulish" — describes the person operating solely by natural human faculties (ψυχή), without the Spirit. This is not "sinful" per se but designates the unregenerate capacity. Contrasted with "πνευματικός" (pneumatikos) in v.15. "ἀνακρίνεται" (anakrinetai, Strong's G350) means "examined" or "discerned," a judicial term used in Athenian courts for preliminary investigation.

verses.flow: But the spiritual man discerns all things, yet he himself is discerned by no one.
For "Who has known the mind of the Lord, that he shall instruct Him?" But we have the mind of Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 40:13 (LXX), where the question concerns the incomprehensibility of YHWH's mind. By substituting "Χριστοῦ" (Christou) for "κυρίου" (kyriou) in the application clause, Paul identifies Christ with the Lord of the Isaiah passage, making a profound Christological claim. THEOLOGICAL: "νοῦν Χριστοῦ ἔχομεν" — "we have the mind of Christ" — asserts that through the Spirit, believers are granted participatory access to the very thinking and purposes of Christ, the divine Lord.

verses.flow: And I, brothers, was not able to speak to you as to spiritual ones, but as to fleshly ones, as to infants in Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sarkinos" (σαρκίνοις, Strong's G4560) means "made of flesh," describing material composition rather than moral disposition—distinct from "sarkikos" (G4559) used in v.3, which implies behaving according to the flesh. Paul softens his rebuke here by using the milder term, acknowledging their genuine but immature state in Christ. "Nēpios" (G3516) denotes a small child or infant, not merely a young believer but one incapable of processing mature spiritual nourishment.

verses.flow: Milk I gave you to drink, not solid food, for you were not yet able; but neither even now are you able,
for you are still fleshly. For where there is among you jealousy and strife, are you not fleshly and walking according to man?
verses.note: GREEK: Here Paul shifts to "sarkikoi" (σαρκικοί, G4559), a stronger term than "sarkinois" in v.1, indicating not merely fleshly composition but fleshly behavior and orientation. "Zēlos" (G2205) can mean zeal positively but here denotes jealousy, and "eris" (G2054) means strife or contention. THEOLOGICAL: Walking "according to man" (kata anthrōpon) stands in stark contrast to walking according to the Spirit (Rom 8:4), revealing that factionalism is evidence of spiritual immaturity rather than sophisticated theological allegiance.

verses.flow: For whenever someone says, "I am of Paul," and another, "I am of Apollos," are you not mere men?
What then is Apollos? And what is Paul? Servants through whom you believed, and to each as the Lord gave.
I planted, Apollos watered, but God was causing the growth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: So then neither the one planting is anything, nor the one watering, but God who causes the growth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now the one planting and the one watering are one, but each will receive his own reward according to his own labor.
For we are God's fellow workers; you are God's field, God's building.
verses.note: GREEK: "Synergoi" (συνεργοί, G4904) means "co-workers" or "fellow workers"—importantly, workers belonging to God (theou is possessive genitive), not workers alongside God as equals. "Geōrgion" (G1091) means cultivated field or farmland, a NT hapax legomenon. "Oikodomē" (G3619) means building or edifice. STRUCTURE: The triple "theou" (God's...God's...God's) creates a powerful rhetorical emphasis: all ownership and agency belongs to God, demolishing the personality cult divisions of vv.3-4.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: According to the grace of God having been given to me, as a wise master builder I laid a foundation, and another builds upon it. But let each one watch how he builds upon it.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For no one is able to lay another foundation beside the one lying there, which is Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The definitive christological declaration of this passage—Jesus Christ is the sole and irreplaceable foundation (themelion, G2310) of the church. No apostle, teacher, or leader can serve as this foundation; they can only build upon it. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Isaiah 28:16 (LXX), where God lays in Zion a chosen, precious cornerstone. Christ fulfills the prophetic foundation stone upon which the eschatological temple-community is built (cf. Eph 2:20, 1 Pet 2:4-6).

verses.flow: Now if anyone builds upon the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble—
each one's work will become manifest, for the Day will disclose it, because it is revealed in fire; and the fire itself will test each one's work, of what sort it is.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Day" (hē hēmera) without further qualification refers to the Day of the Lord—the eschatological day of Christ's judgment (cf. 1 Cor 4:3-5, 2 Cor 5:10). GREEK: "Apokalyptetai" (G601) means "is revealed" or "is uncovered"—the Day's fire-nature is presently being disclosed as an eschatological reality. "Dokimasei" (G1381) means to test or assay, as a metallurgist tests metals by fire, distinguishing precious materials from worthless ones. This is evaluative judgment of believers' works, not condemnatory judgment of their persons.

verses.flow: If anyone's work which he built upon it remains, he will receive a reward.
If anyone's work is burned up, he will suffer loss; but he himself will be saved, yet so as through fire.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse clarifies that the fire tests works, not persons. The builder whose work is consumed "will suffer loss" (zēmiōthēsetai, G2210, a passive indicating forfeiture of reward) but "he himself will be saved" (sōthēsetai). Salvation remains secure through Christ the foundation (v.11), but reward is contingent on faithful labor. The phrase "as through fire" (hōs dia pyros) evokes the image of one escaping a burning building—saved, but barely, with nothing to show for one's labor.

verses.flow: Do you not know that you are a temple of God, and the Spirit of God dwells in you?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Naos" (ναός, G3485) refers specifically to the inner sanctuary or holy of holies, not the broader temple complex (hieron). Paul identifies the corporate congregation—"you" is plural—as the very dwelling place of God's Spirit, transferring temple theology from the Jerusalem structure to the believing community. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The indwelling of God's Spirit in the community echoes Ezekiel 37:27 and the eschatological promise that God would dwell among His people. The church as naos theou represents the fulfillment of the divine habitation motif running from Eden through tabernacle through temple.

verses.flow: If anyone destroys the temple of God, God will destroy this one; for the temple of God is holy, which you are.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phtheirei...phtherei" (φθείρει...φθερεῖ) uses the same root verb (G5351) in present and future tenses, creating a lex talionis wordplay: "destroys...will destroy." The term encompasses corruption, ruin, and moral defilement. THEOLOGICAL: Because the community is God's holy sanctuary (hagios), to corrupt or divide it through factionalism (the issue since 1:10) constitutes a profanation of sacred space. God's response will be commensurate with the offense—this is among the sternest warnings in Paul's letters.

verses.flow: Let no one deceive himself. If anyone thinks himself to be wise among you in this age, let him become foolish, so that he may become wise.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the wisdom of this world is foolishness before God. For it is written: "He who catches the wise in their craftiness,"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Job 5:13 (the only direct NT quotation from Job), adapting the LXX slightly. "Drassomenos" (δρασσόμενος, G1405) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to catch" or "to grasp" as one seizes something by the hand. God ensnares the worldly-wise in the very schemes they devise. THEOLOGICAL: The juxtaposition of divine and worldly wisdom, central to 1 Corinthians 1-3, reaches its climax here: what the world esteems as sophisticated is, before God, foolishness (mōria, G3472).

verses.flow: and again, "The Lord knows the reasonings of the wise, that they are futile."
So then, let no one boast in men. For all things are yours,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas, whether the world or life or death, whether things present or things to come—all things are yours,
and you are of Christ, and Christ is of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The great chain of belonging: believers belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God. This is not a statement of ontological subordination but of mediatorial order—Christ as the Anointed One fulfills His mission in perfect unity with the Father (cf. 1 Cor 11:3, 15:28). STRUCTURE: This verse concludes the entire argument begun in 1:10. The Corinthians were boasting "I am of Paul" (1:12); Paul reframes all reality: all things—apostles, cosmos, life, death—belong to them in Christ, and they belong to Christ, and Christ to God. The factional hierarchy is demolished by a cosmic one.

verses.flow: Thus let a man regard us, as servants of Christ and stewards of the mysteries of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Servants" (ὑπηρέτας, hypēretas, Strong's G5257) originally denoted an "under-rower" on a galley ship, hence a subordinate who serves under authority. "Stewards" (οἰκονόμους, oikonomous, G3623) refers to a household manager entrusted with the master's estate. "Mysteries" (μυστηρίων, mystēriōn, G3466) in Pauline usage denotes truths once hidden but now revealed through Christ (Eph 3:3-6), not esoteric secrets.

verses.flow: Here, moreover, it is required among stewards that one be found faithful.
But to me it is a very small thing that I should be examined by you or by a human day; yet neither do I examine myself.
verses.note: GREEK: "Human day" (ἀνθρωπίνης ἡμέρας) is a striking and unusual expression, contrasting any merely human tribunal or "day of judgment" with the Lord's own day of evaluation (v. 5). Paul relativizes all human assessment—including self-assessment—before the coming judgment of the Lord. THEOLOGICAL: This establishes that ultimate justification of one's ministry belongs to Christ alone, not to any human court of opinion.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I am conscious of nothing against myself, yet I am not by this justified; but the one examining me is the Lord.

Therefore judge nothing before the time, until the Lord comes, who will both bring to light the hidden things of darkness and will make manifest the counsels of the hearts; and then the praise shall come to each one from God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul points to the Parousia as the definitive moment of evaluation. The Lord will "bring to light" (φωτίσει) what is hidden in darkness and "make manifest" (φανερώσει) the counsels of hearts—a function belonging to God alone (Ps 139:1-4; Jer 17:10). This affirms Christ's divine prerogative of omniscient judgment. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of hidden things brought to light before a divine tribunal echoes the heavenly court scene where God judges the inner realities of beings (Dan 7:9-10).

verses.flow: Now these things, brothers, I have applied in figure to myself and Apollos for your sake, that in us you might learn the saying, "Not beyond what is written," that none of you be puffed up on behalf of the one against the other.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For who distinguishes you? And what do you have that you did not receive? But if indeed you received it, why do you boast as though not having received?

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Already you are filled! Already you have become rich! Apart from us you have reigned! And would that you did indeed reign, so that we also might reign together with you.
For I think that God has exhibited us the apostles last of all, as men appointed to death, because we have become a spectacle to the world, both to angels and to men.
verses.note: GREEK: "Appointed to death" (ἐπιθανατίους, epithanatious) is a NT hapax legomenon, referring to condemned criminals paraded last in a Roman triumphal procession to face death in the arena. "Spectacle" (θέατρον, theatron, G2302) evokes the Roman amphitheater. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The audience comprises "the world, both angels and men"—Paul envisions a cosmic theater in which the apostolic suffering is witnessed by the entire created order, including heavenly beings (Eph 3:10).

verses.flow: We are fools for the sake of Christ, but you are wise in Christ; we are weak, but you are strong; you are honored, but we are dishonored.
Until the present hour we both hunger and thirst, and are poorly clothed, and are buffeted, and are without a settled dwelling,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and we toil, working with our own hands. Being reviled, we bless; being persecuted, we endure;

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being slandered, we entreat. We have become as the refuse of the world, the offscouring of all things, until now.
verses.note: GREEK: "Refuse" (περικαθάρματα, perikatharmata) and "offscouring" (περίψημα, peripsēma) are rare terms. Perikatharmata was used of human scapegoats in Greek purification rituals—persons sacrificed or expelled to cleanse a city. Peripsēma (hapax in NT) means "that which is wiped off," i.e., filth scraped away. TYPOLOGY: The scapegoat imagery echoes the Levitical sin-bearing ritual (Lev 16:21-22) and anticipates the pattern of Christ who became a curse for us (Gal 3:13).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I write these things not shaming you, but as my beloved children I admonish you.
For if you should have ten thousand tutors in Christ, yet not many fathers; for in Christ Jesus, through the gospel, I begot you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Tutors" (παιδαγωγούς, paidagōgous, G3807) refers not to teachers but to the household slave who supervised and escorted a child to school—a custodian, not a father. The contrast is between countless guardians and one spiritual father. "I begot you" (ἐγέννησα, egennēsa) uses the aorist of γεννάω, indicating Paul's unique role as their spiritual progenitor through the gospel (cf. Gal 4:19; Philem 10). TYPOLOGY: Paul mirrors the fatherhood of God himself, who begets children through the word of truth (Jas 1:18).

verses.flow: I entreat you therefore, become imitators of me.
For this reason I have sent to you Timothy, who is my beloved and faithful child in the Lord, who will remind you of my ways which are in Christ Jesus, just as I teach everywhere in every assembly.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Now some have been puffed up, as though I were not coming to you.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But I will come to you shortly, if the Lord wills, and I will know not the word of those who have been puffed up, but the power.
For the kingdom of God is not in word, but in power.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The kingdom of God is not in word but in power" (δυνάμει, dynamei, G1411) encapsulates a core Pauline principle: the reign of God manifests not through eloquent rhetoric (cf. 1 Cor 2:4) but through the transformative, supernatural power of the Spirit. This directly counters the Corinthian fascination with rhetorical skill and wisdom of speech (1 Cor 1:17; 2:1-5).

verses.flow: What do you desire? Shall I come to you with a rod, or in love and a spirit of gentleness?
verses.note: GREEK: "Rod" (ῥάβδῳ, rhabdō, G4464) evokes the disciplinary staff of a father or ruler. "Spirit of gentleness" (πνεύματί πραΰτητος) may denote either the human spirit characterized by gentleness or the Holy Spirit producing gentleness as fruit (Gal 5:23). TYPOLOGY: The rod echoes the shepherd-king imagery of the Psalms (Ps 2:9; Ps 23:4), where the same ruler both disciplines and comforts—Paul presents himself as a father wielding paternal authority under Christ the King.

verses.flow: It is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among you, and such sexual immorality as is not even among the nations — so that someone has his father's wife.
verses.note: GREEK: πορνεία (porneía, Strong's G4202) denotes sexual immorality broadly, here specifying an incestuous union. "His father's wife" (γυναῖκά τινα τοῦ πατρός) likely refers to a stepmother, not the man's biological mother. HISTORICAL: Such a union was forbidden both in the Torah (Lev 18:8; Deut 22:30) and in Roman law. That Paul says it is "not even among the nations" (ἔθνεσιν) indicates this transgressed even pagan moral boundaries.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And you are puffed up, and did not rather mourn, so that the one having done this deed might be removed from your midst.
verses.note: GREEK: πεφυσιωμένοι (pephysiōménoi, perfect passive participle of φυσιόω, G5448) means "having been inflated, puffed up" — a term Paul uses repeatedly against Corinthian arrogance (1 Cor 4:6, 18, 19). The perfect tense indicates a settled state of pride. THEOLOGICAL: Rather than grief leading to discipline, the congregation's pride prevented the exercise of covenantal accountability.

verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I indeed, being absent in the body but present in the spirit, have already judged, as being present, the one having so worked out this thing —

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: in the name of our Lord Jesus, you being gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus,
to deliver such a one to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that the spirit might be saved in the day of the Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Deliver to Satan" (παραδοῦναι τῷ Σατανᾷ) describes formal expulsion from the covenant community into the domain of the adversary (cf. 1 Tim 1:20). The purpose is remedial, not final — "destruction of the flesh" (ὄλεθρον τῆς σαρκός) aims at the salvation of the spirit. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan here functions within a bounded role under divine sovereignty; the assembly acts with the delegated authority and power of the Lord Jesus (v. 4), exercising judgment that echoes the heavenly court.

verses.flow: Your boasting is not good. Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump?
Cleanse out the old leaven, so that you may be a new lump, just as you are unleavened. For indeed Christ our Passover has been sacrificed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul identifies Christ directly as "our Passover" (τὸ πάσχα ἡμῶν), the antitype of the Exodus lamb (Exod 12:3-13). The verb ἐτύθη (etýthē, aorist passive of θύω, G2380, "was sacrificed") points to the completed sacrificial death of Christ. The command to "cleanse out the old leaven" (ἐκκαθάρατε) echoes the pre-Passover purging of leaven from Israelite homes (Exod 12:15; 13:7), now applied to the moral purification of the church as the new covenant community.

verses.flow: So then, let us keep the feast, not with old leaven, nor with the leaven of malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.
verses.note: GREEK: εἰλικρίνεια (eilikríneia, G1505) means "sincerity, purity" — etymologically related to being tested by sunlight (εἵλη + κρίνω), suggesting that which is judged pure when examined in the light. TYPOLOGY: The Feast of Unleavened Bread (Exod 12:15-20) becomes an ongoing metaphor for the ethical life of believers, whose perpetual feast is grounded in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice.

verses.flow: I wrote to you in my letter not to associate with sexually immoral persons —
not at all meaning the sexually immoral of this world, or the covetous and swindlers, or idolaters, since then you would need to go out of the world.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But now I have written to you not to associate with anyone who is called a brother if he is a sexually immoral person, or covetous, or an idolater, or a reviler, or a drunkard, or a swindler — with such a one not even to eat.
verses.note: GREEK: συναναμίγνυσθαι (synanamígnysthai, G4874) is a compound verb meaning "to mix up together with," denoting intimate social association. The prohibition μηδὲ συνεσθίειν ("not even to eat with") likely includes exclusion from the Lord's Supper as well as table fellowship. HISTORICAL: In the ancient world, sharing a meal signified social solidarity and covenant bond; refusal to eat together constituted a powerful act of communal discipline.

verses.flow: For what is it to me to judge those outside? Is it not those inside whom you judge?
But those outside, God judges. "Remove the evil person from among yourselves."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The closing command "Remove the evil person from among yourselves" (ἐξάρατε τὸν πονηρὸν ἐξ ὑμῶν αὐτῶν) is a direct citation from the LXX refrain in Deuteronomy (Deut 17:7; 19:19; 22:21, 24; 24:7), where Israel is commanded to purge covenant-breaking evil from its midst. Paul applies the Deuteronomic community discipline formula directly to the church, indicating continuity between Israel and the ekklēsia as God's covenant assembly under divine law.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Does anyone of you, having a matter against the other, dare to be judged before the unrighteous, and not before the saints?
Or do you not know that the saints shall judge the world? And if the world is judged by you, are you unworthy of the least tribunals?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul grounds the saints' authority to judge mundane disputes in the eschatological reality that the holy ones (hoi hagioi) will judge the cosmos. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This echoes Daniel 7:22 where judgment is given to the "saints of the Most High," placing believers within the divine council's judicial function. The word kritērion (Strong's G2922) means "tribunal" or "law court," not merely a case or dispute.

verses.flow: Do you not know that we shall judge angels? How much more, matters of this life?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The judging of angels (angelous, G32) reflects the Second Temple Jewish understanding that rebellious members of the heavenly host would face eschatological judgment. This connects to traditions found in 1 Enoch and Jude 6, where fallen angelic beings are reserved for judgment. Paul's argument moves from the greater (cosmic, angelic judgment) to the lesser (biōtika, G982 — matters pertaining to daily life).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: If then you have tribunals concerning matters of this life, do you seat those who are despised in the assembly?
I say this to your shame. Is it so, that there is not among you one wise man who shall be able to decide between his brother?
But brother goes to judgment against brother, and this before unbelievers!
Already then it is altogether a defeat for you, that you have lawsuits with one another. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?
But you yourselves wrong and defraud, and this your own brothers!

Or do you not know that the unrighteous shall not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived: neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor the effeminate, nor men who lie with men,
verses.note: GREEK: Arsenokoitai (G733) is a compound word from arsēn ("male") and koitē ("bed"), likely coined from the Septuagint of Leviticus 18:22 and 20:13. Malakoi (G3120) literally means "soft ones" and in this context refers to the passive partner in sexual immorality. THEOLOGICAL: The vice list functions as a covenant boundary marker — those whose lives are characterized by such practices stand outside the inheritance of God's kingdom (basileian theou).

verses.flow: nor thieves, nor the covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor swindlers shall inherit the kingdom of God.
And such were some of you. But you were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and in the Spirit of our God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triple "but" (alla...alla...alla) marks a dramatic rhetorical contrast between the believers' former identity and their transformed state. Three verbs describe salvation comprehensively: apelousasthe ("you were washed," G628, middle voice — baptismal imagery), hēgiasthēte ("you were sanctified," G37, set apart as holy), edikaiōthēte ("you were justified," G1344, declared righteous). All three are aorist, pointing to a decisive past act accomplished in the name of the Lord Jesus and in the Spirit of God — a proto-Trinitarian formula.

verses.flow: All things are lawful for me, but not all things are profitable. All things are lawful for me, but I will not be mastered by anything.
Foods are for the stomach and the stomach for foods, but God will abolish both this and these. Now the body is not for sexual immorality but for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.
And God both raised the Lord and will raise us up through His power.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The resurrection of the Lord Jesus serves as the firstfruits pattern for the bodily resurrection of believers. God (ho theos) raised (ēgeiren, aorist) the Lord, and will raise (exegerei, future) us — connecting Christology and eschatology. Paul anchors bodily ethics in resurrection hope: the body matters because God will raise it.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then, having taken the members of Christ, make them members of a harlot? May it never be!

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Or do you not know that the one who is joined to the harlot is one body? For "the two," He says, "shall become one flesh."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 (LXX) — "the two shall become one flesh" (esontai hoi dyo eis sarka mian). The original creation ordinance of marriage is here applied negatively to warn against sexual union with a harlot. The verb kollōmenos (G2853, "being joined/cleaved") is the same verb used in the LXX of Genesis 2:24, showing that sexual union creates a real ontological bond.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one who is joined to the Lord is one spirit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: In direct parallel contrast to verse 16, the one joined (kollōmenos) to the Lord becomes "one spirit" (hen pneuma). This is one of the most profound statements of mystical union with Christ in the Pauline corpus. The union with Christ is spiritual in nature, transcending and surpassing the physical union described in the preceding verse.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Flee sexual immorality. Every sin that a man commits is outside the body, but the one who commits sexual immorality sins against his own body.
Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have from God, and you are not your own?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul shifts from the corporate temple image (1 Cor 3:16, where the church is God's temple) to the individual body as a naos (G3485) — the inner sanctuary, the holy of holies where God's presence dwells. TYPOLOGY: The believer's body fulfills the temple typology: as God's glory (Shekinah) filled the tabernacle and temple, so the Holy Spirit now indwells each believer. The statement "you are not your own" demolishes any notion of autonomous bodily self-determination.

verses.flow: For you were bought with a price; therefore glorify God in your body.
verses.note: GREEK: Ēgorasthēte (G59, aorist passive of agorazō) means "you were purchased" — marketplace language drawn from the slave trade or the redemption of prisoners. Timēs (G5092, "with a price") is a genitive of price, emphasizing the costliness of the ransom paid. THEOLOGICAL: The price is Christ's blood (cf. 1 Pet 1:18-19; Acts 20:28). Paul's ethical imperative — "glorify God in your body" — flows directly from this indicative of redemption. Believers are God's purchased possession; bodily holiness is therefore an act of worship.

verses.flow: Now concerning the things about which you wrote: it is good for a man not to touch a woman.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb haptō (ἅπτεσθαι, Strong's G681) here means "to touch, to fasten to," and is a euphemism for sexual relations. STRUCTURE: This verse marks a transition; Paul now responds to specific questions the Corinthians wrote to him. The phrase "Concerning the things about which you wrote" (Περὶ δὲ ὧν ἐγράψατε) introduces a recurring formula throughout the letter (cf. 7:25, 8:1, 12:1, 16:1).

verses.flow: But on account of fornications, let each man have his own wife, and let each woman have her own husband.
verses.note: GREEK: Porneía (πορνεία, Strong's G4202) denotes sexual immorality broadly, not merely prostitution. The plural "fornications" (τὰς πορνείας) indicates various forms of sexual sin prevalent in Corinth. HISTORICAL: Corinth was infamous in the ancient world for sexual licentiousness; the verb "to Corinthianize" (κορινθιάζεσθαι) meant to practice immorality.

verses.flow: Let the husband render to the wife the debt owed, and likewise also the wife to the husband.
The wife does not have authority over her own body, but the husband does; and likewise also the husband does not have authority over his own body, but the wife does.
verses.note: GREEK: Exousiázō (ἐξουσιάζει, Strong's G1850) means "to exercise authority over." THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes a remarkably mutual and reciprocal view of conjugal authority — the husband does not have unilateral authority over the wife's body nor she over his. This symmetrical framing was radical in the Greco-Roman context.

verses.flow: Do not deprive one another, unless perhaps by agreement for a time, that you may devote yourselves to prayer, and come together again upon the same place, lest Satan tempt you on account of your lack of self-control.
verses.note: GREEK: Symphōnou (συμφώνου, Strong's G4859) means "by agreement, by consent" — the root from which English "symphony" derives. Akrasía (ἀκρασία, Strong's G192) means "lack of self-control, incontinence." THEOLOGICAL: Satan (ὁ Σατανᾶς) is depicted as actively exploiting moral weakness, consistent with the adversarial role described throughout Scripture (cf. Matt 4:3; 1 Thess 3:5).

verses.flow: But this I say by way of concession, not by way of command.
Yet I wish all men to be even as I myself am; but each has his own gift from God, one indeed in this manner, and another in that manner.
verses.note: GREEK: Chárisma (χάρισμα, Strong's G5486) means a "grace-gift," a gift freely bestowed. Paul views both marriage and celibacy as genuine gifts (charismata) from God, not merely human choices. THEOLOGICAL: This verse implies that the capacity for celibacy is a supernatural endowment, not an inherent virtue achievable by willpower alone.

verses.flow: But I say to the unmarried and to the widows: it is good for them if they remain even as I am.
But if they do not exercise self-control, let them marry; for it is better to marry than to burn.
verses.note: GREEK: Pyroûsthai (πυροῦσθαι) means "to burn," here used metaphorically for burning with unfulfilled sexual desire. Kreîtton (κρεῖττον) is the comparative of "good" — "better." THEOLOGICAL: Paul does not denigrate marriage; rather, he presents it as the proper and honorable outlet for desire, guarding against both ascetic extremism and moral laxity.

words.m: verb perfect active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: But to those who have married I charge — not I, but the Lord — a wife is not to be separated from her husband;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes between his own apostolic counsel and a direct command of the Lord Jesus (cf. vv. 12, 25, 40). The charge here likely references Jesus' teaching on divorce (Matt 5:32; 19:6; Mark 10:9-12; Luke 16:18). This distinction between dominical command and apostolic guidance is unique and significant for understanding biblical authority.

verses.flow: but if indeed she is separated, let her remain unmarried, or let her be reconciled to her husband — and a husband is not to send away his wife.
But to the rest I say — I, not the Lord — if any brother has a wife who is an unbeliever, and she consents to dwell with him, let him not send her away.
And a wife who has a husband who is an unbeliever, and he consents to dwell with her, let her not send away her husband.

For the unbelieving husband has been sanctified through the wife, and the unbelieving wife has been sanctified through the brother; since otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy.
verses.note: GREEK: Hēgíastai (ἡγίασται, Strong's G37) is perfect passive of hagiázō, "has been sanctified" — indicating a completed state with ongoing results. THEOLOGICAL: The sanctification here is covenantal and positional, not salvific — the unbelieving spouse is set apart in a special relational proximity to the holy community. The children are called "holy" (hágia), reflecting Old Testament concepts of covenant holiness extending to households (cf. Gen 17:7; Acts 16:31).

verses.flow: But if the unbelieving one departs, let him depart. The brother or the sister has not been bound in such cases; but God has called you in peace.
verses.note: GREEK: Dedoúlōtai (δεδούλωται, Strong's G1402) is perfect passive of doulóō, "has been enslaved/bound." THEOLOGICAL: This is the so-called "Pauline privilege" — the believing spouse is not bound as a slave when the unbeliever willfully departs. The call to peace (eirḗnē) echoes the Hebraic concept of shalom as the governing principle of the believer's life.

verses.flow: For how do you know, O wife, whether you will save your husband? Or how do you know, O husband, whether you will save your wife?
Only as the Lord has apportioned to each, as God has called each one, so let him walk. And so I order in all the assemblies.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Was anyone called having been circumcised? Let him not become uncircumcised. Has anyone been called in uncircumcision? Let him not be circumcised.
Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul relativizes the most fundamental identity marker of Second Temple Judaism — circumcision — declaring it nothing compared to keeping God's commandments. This is a radical reorientation of covenantal identity around obedience rather than ethnic markers (cf. Gal 5:6, 6:15; Rom 2:25-29). TYPOLOGY: The shift from external sign to heart-obedience fulfills the prophetic vision of Deut 30:6 and Jer 31:33.

verses.flow: Each one in the calling in which he was called, in this let him remain.
Were you called as a bondservant? Let it not be a concern to you; but if indeed you are able to become free, rather make use of it.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one called in the Lord as a bondservant is a freedman of the Lord; likewise the one called as a free man is a bondservant of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: Apeleútheros (ἀπελεύθερος, Strong's G558) is a technical Roman legal term for a freedman — a former slave who has been manumitted. THEOLOGICAL: Paul creates a profound paradox: the slave called in Christ is the Lord's freedman; the free person called is Christ's bondservant (doûlos). All social distinctions are relativized under the lordship of Christ (cf. Gal 3:28; Philem 16).

verses.flow: You were bought with a price; do not become bondservants of men.
verses.note: GREEK: Timēs ēgorásthēte (τιμῆς ἠγοράσθητε) — "you were bought with a price." The verb agorázō (Strong's G59) is a marketplace term for purchasing. THEOLOGICAL: This echoes 1 Cor 6:20 and establishes the redemption metaphor: believers have been purchased out of the slave-market of sin by Christ's blood (cf. 1 Pet 1:18-19; Rev 5:9). The implication is exclusive ownership by Christ, precluding voluntary enslavement to human masters or systems.

verses.flow: Each one, brothers, in that condition in which he was called, in this let him remain before God.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now concerning virgins, I have no command of the Lord, but I give my judgment as one who has received mercy from the Lord to be faithful.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: I consider therefore this to be good on account of the present distress: that it is good for a man to remain as he is.
verses.note: GREEK: Enestōsan anánkēn (ἐνεστῶσαν ἀνάγκην) — "the present distress." Anánkē (Strong's G318) denotes compulsion, necessity, or affliction. HISTORICAL: This may refer to a specific famine or persecution affecting Corinth, or more broadly to the eschatological tribulations Paul expected before Christ's return. The phrase colors all of Paul's practical advice in this chapter with eschatological urgency.

verses.flow: Have you been bound to a wife? Do not seek release. Have you been released from a wife? Do not seek a wife.
But if indeed you marry, you have not sinned; and if a virgin marries, she has not sinned. Yet such ones will have affliction in the flesh, and I would spare you.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But this I say, brothers: the time has been shortened; so that from now on, even those having wives should be as those not having them,
verses.note: GREEK: Synestalmenos (συνεσταλμένος, Strong's G4958) means "shortened, compressed, contracted" — the time has been drawn together like a furled sail. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's eschatological framework governs his ethics: because the present age is passing away, all earthly attachments — marriage, grief, joy, commerce — must be held with an open hand. This is not world-denial but eschatological reorientation of priorities.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and those weeping as not weeping, and those rejoicing as not rejoicing, and those buying as not possessing,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and those making use of the world as not making full use of it; for the present form of this world is passing away.
verses.note: GREEK: Schēma (σχῆμα, Strong's G4976) means "outward form, figure, fashion" — the external configuration of the world. Parágei (παράγει) means "is passing by, is passing away." THEOLOGICAL: The present world-order is transient in its form, not illusory but temporary. This undergirds the entire chapter's counsel: earthly conditions are penultimate, not ultimate (cf. 1 John 2:17; 2 Pet 3:10-13).

verses.flow: But I desire you to be free from care. The unmarried man is concerned about the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord;

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but the one who has married is concerned about the things of the world, how he may please his wife,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: and he is divided. And the unmarried woman and the virgin is concerned about the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit; but the one who has married is concerned about the things of the world, how she may please her husband.
And this I say for your own benefit, not that I may cast a snare upon you, but for that which is seemly and devoted to the Lord without distraction.
verses.note: GREEK: Eupáredron (εὐπάρεδρον) — a rare word meaning "devoted attendance upon, sitting well beside." Some manuscripts read euprosedron. Aperispástōs (ἀπερισπάστως, Strong's G563) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "without distraction, without being pulled away." THEOLOGICAL: Paul's ultimate aim is not asceticism but undivided devotion to the Lord — the same principle Martha was reminded of in Luke 10:40-42.

verses.flow: But if anyone thinks he is behaving improperly toward his virgin, if she is past the bloom of youth, and so it ought to be, let him do what he wishes; he does not sin. Let them marry.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he who stands firm in his heart, having no necessity, but has authority over his own will, and has determined this in his own heart, to keep his own virgin, he will do well.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, both he who gives his own virgin in marriage does well, and he who does not give her in marriage will do better.
A wife is bound for as long a time as her husband lives; but if the husband falls asleep, she is free to be married to whom she wishes, only in the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: Koimēthē (κοιμηθῇ) — "falls asleep," the standard early Christian euphemism for death (cf. 1 Thess 4:13-15; John 11:11). THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "only in the Lord" (monon en kyríō) establishes the principle that a believing widow's remarriage must be to a fellow believer. This is one of the clearest NT statements on the expectation of marriage within the community of faith.

verses.flow: But she is more blessed if she remains as she is, according to my judgment; and I think that I also have the Spirit of God.
verses.note: GREEK: Dokō kagō pneûma theoû échein (δοκῶ κἀγὼ πνεῦμα θεοῦ ἔχειν) — "I think that I also have the Spirit of God." The verb dokō can carry a note of modest understatement or ironic litotes. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's closing claim to possess the Spirit of God is likely a subtle rebuke of those in Corinth who claimed superior spiritual authority. His apostolic judgment, though offered as counsel rather than command, carries pneumatic weight (cf. 1 Cor 2:16; 14:37).

verses.flow: Now concerning the things sacrificed to idols, we know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
verses.note: GREEK: "Eidōlothyton" (εἰδωλόθυτον, Strong's G1494) is a compound of "eidōlon" (idol) and "thytón" (sacrificed), referring to meat from pagan temple sacrifices sold in the marketplace. "Physioō" (φυσιόω, G5448) means to inflate or puff up like a bellows — Paul contrasts the inflating effect of mere knowledge with the constructive ("oikodomeō," G3618, to build as a house) work of love. HISTORICAL: Corinth's meat markets were closely tied to pagan temples; virtually all publicly sold meat had been ritually offered to a deity.

verses.flow: If anyone supposes that he has come to know something, he does not yet know as it is necessary to know.
But if anyone loves God, this one has been known by Him.
Concerning therefore the eating of things sacrificed to idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is no God except One.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul affirms the Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4 — "there is no God except One" — while simultaneously acknowledging the reality of spiritual beings called "gods" in the next verse. This is not contradiction but reflects a divine council worldview: lesser spiritual beings exist but possess no ultimate deity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term "eidōlon" (εἴδωλον, G1497) denotes not mere nothingness but a false image; the idol as a physical object is nothing, yet the powers behind pagan worship are real (cf. 1 Cor 10:20, Deut 32:17, Ps 82:1).

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or upon earth — as indeed there are many gods and many lords —
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Paul acknowledges beings "called gods" (legómenoi theoí) in heaven and on earth, echoing Psalm 82 where God stands in the assembly of the "elohim." The phrase "as indeed there are many gods and many lords" (hōsper eisin theoí polloí) is not a concession to polytheism but a recognition that real spiritual entities — members of the divine council who rebelled — stand behind pagan worship. Paul will make this explicit in 1 Cor 10:20. TYPOLOGY: This passage reframes Deuteronomy 32:8-9 and Psalm 82:1-6 within a Christological monotheism.

verses.flow: yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and we unto Him, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and we through Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is a stunning reformulation of the Shema (Deut 6:4). Paul splits the confession between "one God, the Father" (source: "ex hou," from whom) and "one Lord, Jesus Christ" (agent: "di' hou," through whom). By placing Jesus within the identity of the one God of Israel as the mediating Lord of creation, Paul makes one of the highest Christological claims in the New Testament. GREEK: "Di' hou ta panta" (through whom are all things) attributes the agency of all creation to Christ (cf. John 1:3, Col 1:16, Heb 1:2).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But the knowledge is not in all. And some, by familiarity with the idol even until now, eat as though it were a thing sacrificed to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
But food will not commend us to God; for neither if we eat do we abound, nor if we do not eat do we lack.
But take heed lest somehow this authority of yours become a stumbling-block to those who are weak.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: For if anyone sees you, the one having knowledge, reclining at table in an idol's temple, will not the conscience of that one, being weak, be built up to eat things sacrificed to idols?
verses.note: GREEK: "Eidōleion" (εἰδωλεῖον, G1493) is a NT hapax legomenon, meaning "idol's temple" or dining hall attached to a pagan shrine. "Katakeimenon" (reclining) refers to the Greco-Roman posture of dining at formal banquets. HISTORICAL: Archaeological evidence from Corinth confirms banquet halls within temple precincts of Asclepius and Demeter where social dining regularly occurred. Paul's concern is that a weaker believer, seeing a knowledgeable Christian reclining in such a setting, would be "built up" (oikodomeō used ironically here) — emboldened not toward faith but toward idolatry.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one who is weak is destroyed by your knowledge — the brother for whom Christ died.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul delivers the devastating climax of his argument: the weak brother "is destroyed" (apollytai, G622, present tense — perishing in an ongoing sense) through your knowledge. The one being ruined is defined by the highest possible category of worth — "the brother for whom Christ died" (di' hon Christos apethanen). To destroy what Christ died to save inverts the entire logic of the cross. This verse establishes the principle that Christian freedom is always subordinated to sacrificial love.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And thus sinning against the brothers and striking their conscience, being weak, you sin against Christ.
Therefore, if food causes my brother to stumble, I will never eat meat unto the age, so that I might not cause my brother to stumble.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ou mē phagō" (οὐ μὴ φάγω) is the strongest possible Greek negation with a subjunctive — "I will absolutely never eat." "Kreas" (κρέας, G2907) means flesh-meat specifically, distinct from "brōma" (food generally). "Eis ton aiōna" (unto the age) means into perpetuity. THEOLOGICAL: Paul concludes with a personal vow modeling the self-limiting love he commands. His freedom is real but willingly surrendered. This becomes a paradigm for all Christian ethics: the strong bear the burden of the weak (cf. Rom 14:13-21, Rom 15:1).

verses.flow: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you not my work in the Lord?
verses.note: GREEK: The fourfold rhetorical question employs the particle οὐκ/οὐχί (ouk/ouchi) expecting affirmative answers. "Apostle" (ἀπόστολος, apostolos, Strong's G652) means "one sent forth." Paul grounds his apostolic authority in his direct encounter with the risen Christ (cf. Acts 9:3-6; 1 Cor 15:8). THEOLOGICAL: Paul's having "seen" (ἑόρακα, heoraka, perfect tense of horaō) Jesus is stated as a settled, continuing reality—a prerequisite for apostleship on par with the Twelve.

verses.flow: If to others I am not an apostle, yet indeed to you I am, for the seal of my apostleship you are in the Lord.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: My defense to those who examine me is this:
Do we not have authority to eat and to drink?
Do we not have authority to lead about a sister as wife, even as the rest of the apostles and the brothers of the Lord and Cephas?
verses.note: GREEK: "A sister as wife" (ἀδελφὴν γυναῖκα, adelphēn gynaika) denotes a believing woman as a spouse. HISTORICAL: "The brothers of the Lord" refers to Jesus' earthly siblings (cf. Matt 13:55; Gal 1:19), confirming they were active in ministry. Cephas (Κηφᾶς, Aramaic for "rock") is Peter, singled out as a prominent example.

verses.flow: Or is it only I and Barnabas who do not have authority to refrain from working?
Who serves as a soldier at his own wages at any time? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat of its fruit? Who shepherds a flock and does not eat of the milk of the flock?
Do I speak these things according to man? Or does not the Law also say these things?

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: For in the Law of Moses it is written: "You shall not muzzle an ox while it is threshing." Is it for the oxen that God is concerned?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Deut 25:4 from the LXX. The command about the ox is read as having a deeper, typological intent—God's concern extends beyond livestock to those who labor in His service. GREEK: κημώσεις (kēmōseis, "you shall muzzle," Strong's G5392) is a future indicative used with οὐ as a prohibition, preserving the LXX command form.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Or does He speak entirely for our sake? For it was written for our sake, because the one who plows ought to plow in hope, and the one who threshes, in hope of partaking.
If we sowed to you the spiritual things, is it a great thing if we shall reap from you the fleshly things?
If others partake of this authority over you, do not we rather? But we did not make use of this authority, but we endure all things, lest we give any hindrance to the gospel of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Hindrance" (ἐγκοπήν, enkopēn, Strong's G1464) is a military term meaning "a cutting into" or "breaking up a road" to impede an advancing army. THEOLOGICAL: Paul's voluntary renunciation of legitimate rights establishes the paradigm that gospel advancement takes priority over personal entitlement—a principle flowing directly from Christ's own self-emptying (Phil 2:6-8).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Do you not know that those who perform the sacred rites eat from the temple, and those who attend upon the altar have their portion with the altar?

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: So also the Lord ordained for those who proclaim the gospel to live from the gospel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Lord ordained" (ὁ κύριος διέταξεν) refers to Jesus' own teaching that laborers deserve their wages (Luke 10:7; Matt 10:10). The verb διατάσσω (diatassō, Strong's G1299) denotes an authoritative command, underscoring Christ's sovereign authority over the ordering of His church.

verses.flow: But I have made use of none of these things. And I did not write these things that it should be so done for me, for it were better for me to die than— no one shall make void my boasting!
For if I proclaim the gospel, there is no boasting for me, for necessity is laid upon me; for woe to me if I do not proclaim the gospel!
For if I do this willingly, I have a reward; but if unwillingly, a stewardship has been entrusted to me.
verses.note: GREEK: "Stewardship" (οἰκονομίαν, oikonomian, Strong's G3622) denotes the administration of a household entrusted to a servant. THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes between voluntary and compelled service. Even if unwilling, the stewardship (oikonomia) has been entrusted (πεπίστευμαι, pepisteumai, divine passive) to him—God Himself is the one who commissioned it (cf. Gal 1:15-16; Eph 3:2).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: What then is my reward? That in proclaiming the gospel I may offer the gospel free of charge, so as not to make full use of my authority in the gospel.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For being free from all, I enslaved myself to all, that I might gain the greater number.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I became to the Jews as a Jew, that I might gain Jews; to those under the Law as one under the Law—not being myself under the Law—that I might gain those under the Law;

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: to those without law as one without law—not being without the law of God but under the law of Christ—that I might gain those without law.
verses.note: GREEK: "Under the law of Christ" (ἔννομος Χριστοῦ, ennomos Christou) is a significant phrase; ἔννομος (Strong's G1772) means "within law" or "legally bound." THEOLOGICAL: Paul is not antinomian—he distinguishes being free from Torah's covenant administration while remaining bound to Christ's authoritative teaching. This reflects the new covenant framework where Christ fulfills and redefines Torah obligation (cf. Gal 6:2; Matt 5:17).

verses.flow: I became to the weak, weak, that I might gain the weak. To all I have become all things, that by all means I might save some.
And all things I do on account of the gospel, that I may become a fellow-partaker of it.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Do you not know that those who run in a stadium all run, but one receives the prize? So run, that you may lay hold of it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The στάδιον (stadion) refers to the footrace at the Isthmian Games held near Corinth every two years, second only to the Olympics in prestige. Paul's Corinthian audience would have immediately recognized this imagery. GREEK: "Lay hold of" (καταλάβητε, katalabēte, Strong's G2638) means to seize or apprehend decisively—an active, aggressive pursuit.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And everyone who contends exercises self-control in all things. They indeed do it that they may receive a perishable crown, but we an imperishable one.
verses.note: GREEK: "Exercises self-control" (ἐγκρατεύεται, enkrateuetai, Strong's G1467) denotes mastery over one's desires and appetites. TYPOLOGY: The "perishable crown" (φθαρτὸν στέφανον) was a wreath of withered celery or pine at the Isthmian Games, contrasted with the "imperishable" (ἄφθαρτον) crown of eternal glory—echoing the eschatological reward motif (cf. 2 Tim 4:8; 1 Pet 5:4; Rev 2:10).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I therefore so run, as not without aim; so I box, as not beating the air.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I discipline my body and bring it into subjection, lest somehow, having preached to others, I myself should become disqualified.
verses.note: GREEK: "I discipline" (ὑπωπιάζω, hypōpiazō, Strong's G5299) literally means "to strike under the eye," a boxing metaphor for giving oneself a black eye—vivid imagery of severe bodily discipline. "Bring into subjection" (δουλαγωγέω, doulagōgeō, Strong's G1396) means "to lead as a slave." "Disqualified" (ἀδόκιμος, adokimos, Strong's G96) means "failing the test" or "rejected after examination." THEOLOGICAL: Paul does not teach loss of salvation but warns against the real danger of being disqualified from effective ministry and reward—a sobering counterpart to his boasting in free service.

verses.flow: For I do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, that our fathers all were under the cloud, and all passed through the sea,
and all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea,
and all ate the same spiritual food,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: and all drank the same spiritual drink; for they were drinking from a spiritual rock following them, and the Rock was the Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul identifies the rock that provided water in the wilderness (Exod 17:6; Num 20:11) as Christ himself, pre-incarnate and accompanying Israel. The adjective pneumatikós (G4152) marks this as spiritual reality behind physical provision. THEOLOGICAL: This is a striking assertion of Christ's pre-existence and active role in Old Testament redemptive history, consistent with divine council Christology where the Son operates as the visible agent of God among his people.

verses.flow: But with most of them God was not well pleased, for they were struck down in the wilderness.
Now these things became types for us, so that we should not be desirers of evil things, even as those also desired.
verses.note: GREEK: The word týpos (G5179) means "pattern, imprint, figure"—a stamp pressed into wax. Paul uses it technically: Israel's wilderness experiences were divinely intended prefigurations, not mere illustrations. TYPOLOGY: This verse establishes apostolic hermeneutical warrant for reading Old Testament narratives as typological patterns that find their anti-type in the New Covenant community.

verses.flow: Neither become idolaters, as some of them, just as it has been written: "The people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play."
Neither let us commit sexual immorality, as some of them committed immorality, and fell in one day twenty-three thousand.
Neither let us put Christ to the test, as some of them tested, and were being destroyed by the serpents.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb ekpeirázō (G1598) is an intensified form of peirázō—"to test thoroughly, to put to the ultimate test." The textual variant reading Christón rather than kýrion is well attested and theologically significant. THEOLOGICAL: Paul identifies the one whom Israel tested in the wilderness as Christ, again affirming his pre-existence and equating him with the Lord (YHWH) of the Exodus narrative (Num 21:5-6).

verses.flow: Neither murmur, even as some of them murmured, and were destroyed by the destroyer.
verses.note: GREEK: Olothreutḗs (G3644) appears only here in the NT—a hapax legomenon meaning "the destroyer." DIVINE_COUNCIL: This likely refers to the destroying angel of Exodus 12:23 and 2 Samuel 24:16, a spiritual agent executing divine judgment. In Second Temple tradition, this figure was associated with angelic beings in God's council carrying out sentences of punishment.

verses.flow: Now these things happened to them as types, and were written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the ages have come.
verses.note: GREEK: The adverb typikōs ("as types, figuratively") modifies synébainen ("were happening"), indicating these events occurred with typological purpose built into them by God. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "the ends of the ages" (ta télē tōn aiṓnōn) places Paul's audience at the eschatological culmination point—all prior ages converge upon those living in the Messianic era.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, let the one thinking himself to stand take heed lest he fall.

No trial has seized you except what is common to man; but faithful is God, who will not permit you to be tried beyond what you are able, but will make with the trial also the way of escape, so that you are able to endure.
Therefore, my beloved, flee from idolatry.
I speak as to wise ones; judge for yourselves what I say.
The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not a sharing in the blood of the Christ? The bread which we break, is it not a sharing in the body of the Christ?
verses.note: GREEK: Koinōnía (G2842) denotes not merely "fellowship" but genuine participation, sharing, communion—a real partaking in the reality signified. THEOLOGICAL: Paul establishes that the eucharistic cup and bread constitute actual participatory union with Christ's blood and body, forming the theological basis for his argument against participating in pagan sacrificial meals. The structure—cup then bread—may reflect early liturgical practice.

verses.flow: Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Look at Israel according to the flesh: are not those eating the sacrifices sharers of the altar?
What then do I say? That idol-meat is anything, or that an idol is anything?
But that the things which they sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons and not to God; and I do not want you to become sharers with demons.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Paul draws on Deuteronomy 32:17 (LXX), where Israel sacrificed to daimónia ("demons")—lesser spiritual beings, not the true God.
