In the divine council framework, these are rebellious members of the heavenly host to whom the nations were allotted (Deut 32:8-9 LXX). THEOLOGICAL: Idolatry is not merely foolishness directed at nothing; it involves real spiritual entities in opposition to God.

verses.flow: You are not able to drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons; you are not able to partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons.
Or do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The verb parazēlóō (G3863, "provoke to jealousy") directly echoes Deuteronomy 32:21, where Israel provoked God to jealousy through false gods. Paul applies YHWH's jealousy language to the Lord Jesus, again identifying Christ with the God of Israel. THEOLOGICAL: This presupposes that divided loyalty between Christ and demons mirrors Israel's covenant unfaithfulness.

verses.flow: All things are lawful, but not all things are profitable; all things are lawful, but not all things build up.
Let no one seek his own, but that of the other.

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Everything being sold in the meat-market, eat, examining nothing on account of conscience;

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: for "the earth is the Lord's, and the fullness thereof."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: A direct quotation of Psalm 24:1 (LXX 23:1), grounding Christian freedom in the Lord's sovereign ownership of all creation. THEOLOGICAL: Since the earth and its fullness belong to the Lord, food is inherently clean—the issue is not the material substance but the relational and covenantal context of its consumption.

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: If any of the unbelievers invites you, and you desire to go, eat everything set before you, examining nothing on account of conscience.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if someone should say to you, "This is offered in sacred sacrifice," do not eat, on account of that one who disclosed it and on account of conscience—
conscience, I say, not your own, but that of the other. For why is my freedom judged by another's conscience?
If I partake with thankfulness, why am I blasphemed over that for which I give thanks?
Whether then you eat, or drink, or whatever you do, do all things unto the glory of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse functions as the summary principle governing the entire discussion of chapters 8-10 regarding food offered to idols, Christian liberty, and communal responsibility. The dóxa theoû ("glory of God") is the ultimate telos orienting all human action—eating, drinking, and every deed. STRUCTURE: This forms the climactic imperative before the closing exhortation of the chapter.

verses.flow: Become without offense both to Jews and to Greeks and to the assembly of God,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: even as I also please all in all things, not seeking my own advantage, but that of the many, that they may be saved.
Become imitators of me, even as I also am of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Imitators" (μιμηταί, mimētai, Strong's G3402) denotes active emulation, not mere admiration. Paul establishes a chain of imitation: the Corinthians imitate Paul, who imitates Christ. This verse properly concludes the argument of chapter 10 and transitions to the new section on worship order.

verses.flow: Now I commend you, that in all things you have remembered me, and even as I delivered them to you, you hold fast the traditions.
But I want you to know that the head of every man is Christ, and the head of woman is the man, and the head of Christ is God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term κεφαλή (kephalē, "head") is used three times to establish a theological hierarchy: God → Christ → man → woman. Whether kephalē here means "authority over" or "source/origin" has been extensively debated. The ordering places Christ under the headship of God the Father, consistent with the Son's economic subordination while not diminishing ontological equality (Phil 2:6). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This ordered structure reflects the heavenly pattern of delegated authority within the divine economy.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Every man praying or prophesying, having something down over his head, dishonors his head.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: But every woman praying or prophesying with her head uncovered dishonors her head, for it is one and the same as having been shaved.
For if a woman is not covered, let her also be shorn; but if it is shameful for a woman to be shorn or shaved, let her be covered.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For a man indeed ought not to cover his head, being the image and glory of God; but the woman is the glory of man.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul appeals to the creation account (Gen 1:26-27), identifying man as the "image and glory" (εἰκὼν καὶ δόξα) of God. The term εἰκών (eikōn, Strong's G1504) is the same word used of Christ as the image of the invisible God (Col 1:15). Woman as the "glory" (δόξα) of man reflects her derivation from man in the Genesis 2 narrative, not an ontological inferiority.

verses.flow: For man is not from woman, but woman from man.
For also man was not created on account of the woman, but woman on account of the man.
On account of this the woman ought to have authority upon her head, because of the angels.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Because of the angels" (διὰ τοὺς ἀγγέλους) is one of the most enigmatic phrases in the Pauline corpus. In Second Temple Jewish tradition, angels were understood as present observers of worship assemblies (cf. Qumran texts 1QSa II.8-9; 1QM VII.6). The phrase ἐξουσίαν ἔχειν ("to have authority") may denote the woman's own authority expressed through the head covering, rather than a sign of submission to another's authority. GREEK: ἐξουσία (exousia, Strong's G1849) fundamentally means "authority" or "right," not "subjection."

verses.flow: Nevertheless, neither is woman apart from man, nor man apart from woman, in the Lord.
For just as the woman is from the man, so also the man is through the woman; but all things are from God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: Judge among yourselves: is it fitting for a woman to pray to God uncovered?
Does not even nature itself teach you that if a man has long hair, it is a dishonor to him?
But if a woman has long hair, it is a glory to her, for her long hair has been given to her in place of a covering.
But if anyone seems to be contentious, we have no such custom, nor do the assemblies of God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now in giving this charge, I do not commend you, because you come together not for the better but for the worse.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: For first indeed, when you come together in the assembly, I hear that divisions exist among you, and in part I believe it.
For there must also be factions among you, so that those who are approved may become manifest among you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: αἱρέσεις (haireseis, Strong's G139) here means "factions" or "divisions of opinion," from which the English "heresy" derives. Paul makes a striking theological claim that factions serve a providential purpose: to reveal those who are δόκιμοι (dokimoi, "approved," "tested and found genuine," Strong's G1384). This reflects the biblical pattern of testing and refinement (Mal 3:2-3; 1 Pet 1:7).

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: When you come together, therefore, upon the same place, it is not to eat the Lord's supper.
For each one takes beforehand his own supper in the eating, and one is hungry while another is drunk.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For do you not have houses in which to eat and to drink? Or do you despise the assembly of God and put to shame those who have nothing? What shall I say to you? Shall I commend you in this? I do not commend you.
For I received from the Lord that which I also delivered to you, that the Lord Jesus, in the night in which He was being betrayed, took bread,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This is the earliest written account of the Lord's Supper, predating the Gospels. Paul claims direct reception "from the Lord" (ἀπὸ τοῦ κυρίου), indicating either direct revelation or authoritative tradition traceable to Jesus Himself. The phrase "in the night in which He was being betrayed" (παρεδίδετο, imperfect passive of παραδίδωμι) conveys the ongoing, unfolding nature of the betrayal. TYPOLOGY: The Passover context links Christ's institution of the supper to the Exodus redemption narrative (Exod 12:1-28).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and having given thanks, He broke it and said, "This is My body, which is for you; do this in remembrance of Me."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Likewise also the cup, after the supping, saying, "This cup is the new covenant in My blood; do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of Me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The new covenant in My blood" (ἡ καινὴ διαθήκη ἐν τῷ ἐμῷ αἵματι) directly fulfills Jeremiah 31:31-34 (LXX Jer 38:31-34). The term διαθήκη (diathēkē, Strong's G1242) means "covenant" in biblical usage, not merely "testament." Christ's blood ratifies the new covenant just as animal blood ratified the Sinai covenant (Exod 24:8). TYPOLOGY: The cup after supping (μετὰ τὸ δειπνῆσαι) was the third cup of the Passover meal, the "cup of blessing" (cf. 1 Cor 10:16).

verses.flow: For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the death of the Lord until He comes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: καταγγέλλετε ("you proclaim," Strong's G2605) makes the Lord's Supper a proclamatory act — an enacted sermon declaring Christ's atoning death. The eschatological horizon "until He comes" (ἄχρι οὗ ἔλθῃ) frames every observance between the cross and the parousia, anchoring the meal in both past accomplishment and future consummation.

verses.flow: Therefore whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord unworthily shall be guilty of the body and the blood of the Lord.
But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of the bread and let him drink of the cup.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one eating and drinking, eats and drinks judgment to himself, not discerning the body.
verses.note: GREEK: διακρίνων (diakrinōn, Strong's G1252) means "discerning" or "distinguishing properly." The failure to "discern the body" (τὸ σῶμα) may refer to the sacramental body of Christ in the supper, the ecclesial body of Christ (the gathered church), or both. The judgment (κρίμα, krima, Strong's G2917) is remedial rather than final, as verse 32 clarifies.

verses.flow: On account of this, many among you are weak and sick, and a considerable number sleep.
But if we were discerning ourselves, we would not be judged.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But being judged by the Lord, we are disciplined, so that we may not be condemned with the world.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul distinguishes between the Lord's discipline (παιδευόμεθα, "we are disciplined/trained," from παιδεύω, Strong's G3811) and final condemnation (κατακριθῶμεν, "condemned") with the world. This reflects the biblical principle that divine chastening is evidence of sonship, not rejection (Prov 3:11-12; Heb 12:5-11). The Lord's corrective judgment upon His people is merciful, preventing them from sharing the world's ultimate condemnation.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, my brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.
If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, so that you may not come together unto judgment. And the remaining matters I will set in order whenever I come.
Now concerning the spiritual gifts, brothers, I do not wish you to be ignorant.
verses.note: GREEK: "Pneumatikōn" (πνευματικῶν, Strong's G4152) can be rendered "spiritual things" or "spiritual gifts" — the genitive plural is ambiguous between neuter ("spiritual things/gifts") and masculine ("spiritual persons"). Context favors neuter, referring to the Spirit-given endowments discussed throughout the chapter. Paul's "I do not wish you to be ignorant" (ou thelō hymas agnoein) is a standard Pauline disclosure formula (cf. Rom 1:13; 2 Cor 1:8; 1 Thess 4:13).

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: You know that when you were Gentiles, you were led away to mute idols, however you were being led.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore I make known to you that no one speaking by the Spirit of God says "Jesus is accursed," and no one is able to say "Jesus is Lord" except by the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes the foundational criterion for discerning the Holy Spirit's work: confession of Christ's lordship. "Anathema" (ἀνάθεμα, G331) is a solemn curse-word drawn from the LXX concept of ḥerem — something devoted to destruction. No one operating under the Spirit of God can pronounce Jesus accursed, and conversely, the genuine confession "Kyrios Iēsous" ("Jesus is Lord") is possible only by the Holy Spirit. This connects to the earliest Christian creed (Rom 10:9; Phil 2:11).

verses.flow: Now there are distributions of gifts, but the same Spirit;
verses.note: GREEK: "Diaireseis" (διαιρέσεις, G1243) means "distributions" or "apportionments," not merely "differences" or "diversities." The word shares its root with "diairoun" in v.11 (distributing). THEOLOGICAL: Verses 4-6 present a proto-Trinitarian structure: the same Spirit (v.4), the same Lord (v.5), the same God (v.6) — Spirit, Son, and Father as the unified source of all varied gifts, ministries, and workings.

verses.flow: and there are distributions of ministries, and the same Lord;

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and there are distributions of workings, but the same God, the one working all things in all.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: But to each one is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common benefit.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phanerōsis" (φανέρωσις, G5321) means "manifestation" or "disclosure" — the Spirit makes himself visible through each believer's gift. "Sympheron" (συμφέρον, G4851) denotes "that which is profitable" or "the common advantage," emphasizing that spiritual gifts serve the corporate body, not individual exaltation.

verses.flow: For to one indeed through the Spirit is given a word of wisdom, and to another a word of knowledge according to the same Spirit,
to another faith by the same Spirit, and to another gifts of healings by the one Spirit,
and to another workings of powers, and to another prophecy, and to another discernings of spirits, and to another kinds of tongues, and to another interpretation of tongues.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: But all these things works the one and the same Spirit, distributing to each one individually just as he wills.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit is here described as sovereign, distributing gifts "just as he wills" (kathōs bouletai). The verb "boulomai" (G1014) denotes deliberate, purposeful willing. This ascribes personal agency and divine sovereignty to the Holy Spirit — he is not an impersonal force but a divine person who exercises his own will in the distribution of gifts (cf. Heb 2:4).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: For just as the body is one and has many members, but all the members of the body, being many, are one body, so also is Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul's striking conclusion — "so also is Christ" rather than "so also is the church" — identifies the corporate body of believers with Christ himself. This echoes the Damascus road encounter where the risen Christ said "Why do you persecute me?" (Acts 9:4), identifying himself with his people. The body metaphor draws on Greco-Roman political rhetoric but is transformed by its christological center.

verses.flow: For indeed by one Spirit we all were baptized into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, whether slaves or free, and we all were given to drink of one Spirit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Baptism by the Spirit into one body transcends the fundamental social divisions of the ancient world: Jew/Greek (ethnic-religious), slave/free (socio-economic). "We were all given to drink" (epotisthēmen) uses the aorist passive of "potizō" (G4222), suggesting a single decisive spiritual reality — perhaps echoing the rock that gave water in the wilderness, which Paul identifies as Christ (1 Cor 10:4). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The one Spirit incorporates all believers into the one body under Christ the head, reconstituting a unified people from every nation.

verses.flow: For indeed the body is not one member but many.
If the foot should say, "Because I am not a hand, I am not of the body," it is not for this reason not of the body.
And if the ear should say, "Because I am not an eye, I am not of the body," it is not for this reason not of the body.
If the whole body were an eye, where would be the hearing? If the whole were hearing, where would be the smelling?
But now God has placed the members, each one of them, in the body just as he willed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God's sovereign design of the body is emphasized: "etheto" (ἔθετο, G5087) means "placed" or "appointed" — the same verb used in v.28 for God's appointment of ministries. Each member's position is by divine intention, not accident. This grounds ecclesial diversity in the will of God the Creator.

verses.flow: And if they were all one member, where would be the body?
But now indeed there are many members, yet one body.
And the eye is not able to say to the hand, "I have no need of you," or again the head to the feet, "I have no need of you."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: But rather, much more the members of the body that seem to be weaker are necessary,
and those which we consider to be less honorable of the body, upon these we bestow more abundant honor, and our uncomely parts have more abundant comeliness,

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular neuter
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but our comely parts have no need. Yet God has composed the body together, having given more abundant honor to the part that lacked,
so that there might be no division in the body, but that the members might have the same care for one another.
And if one member suffers, all the members suffer together; if one member is glorified, all the members rejoice together.
Now you are the body of Christ, and members individually.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ek merous" (ἐκ μέρους) literally "out of a part" — meaning "individually" or "severally," indicating that each believer constitutes a particular member of Christ's body. THEOLOGICAL: This is the thesis statement for the entire analogy: the Corinthian congregation corporately is "body of Christ" (sōma Christou, without the article, emphasizing quality) and individually members of it.

verses.flow: And God has appointed in the assembly first apostles, second prophets, third teachers, then powers, then gifts of healings, helps, administrations, kinds of tongues.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Paul lists eight ministry functions in a deliberate order: the first three are ranked numerically (first, second, third), then the remaining five are introduced with "then" (epeita). GREEK: "Antilempseis" (ἀντιλήμψεις, G484) means "helpful deeds" or "acts of assistance" — a NT hapax legomenon. "Kybernēseis" (κυβερνήσεις, G2941) means "acts of governance" or "administrations," from the word for a ship's helmsman — also a NT hapax. HISTORICAL: The ranking of apostles, prophets, and teachers corresponds to the foundational leadership of the early church (cf. Eph 2:20; 4:11).

verses.flow: Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Are all workers of powers?
Do all have gifts of healings? Do all speak with tongues? Do all interpret?
But earnestly desire the greater gifts. And yet I show you a still more surpassing way.
verses.note: GREEK: "Zēloute" (ζηλοῦτε, G2206) is an imperative — "earnestly desire" or "be zealous for." "Kath' hyperbolēn hodon" (καθ' ὑπερβολὴν ὁδόν) means literally "a way according to excess/surpassing" — a superlative expression. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the hinge between the gifts discussion (ch. 12) and the supreme "way" of love (ch. 13), which Paul will present not as a gift but as the indispensable manner in which all gifts must operate.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: If with the tongues of men I speak and of angels, but love I have not, I have become a sounding brass or a clanging cymbal.
verses.note: GREEK: "Agapē" (ἀγάπη, Strong's G26) is the key term dominating this chapter, occurring nine times. It denotes self-giving, covenantal love rooted in the will rather than mere emotion. "Alalazōn" (ἀλαλάζον, G214) means "clanging" or "wailing" — used of ritual pagan cymbal-crashing, likely evoking Corinthian cult worship practices. Paul contrasts ecstatic spiritual performance devoid of love with empty cultic noise. HISTORICAL: Corinth hosted temples where ecstatic speech and clashing instruments featured in pagan rites; Paul's imagery would strike close to home.

verses.flow: And if I have prophecy and know the mysteries all and all the knowledge, and if I have all the faith so as to remove mountains, but love I have not, I am nothing.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystēria" (μυστήρια, G3466) refers to divine secrets hidden in God's counsel now revealed through the Spirit (cf. Eph 3:3-5; Rom 16:25). "Methistanai" (μεθιστάναι, G3179) means "to move from one place to another, to remove." THEOLOGICAL: Paul lists the highest spiritual gifts — prophecy, knowledge of all mysteries, mountain-moving faith (cf. Matt 17:20) — and declares them valueless apart from love. Even participation in divine council revelation profits nothing without agapē.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And if I give away all my possessions to feed the poor, and if I hand over my body that I may be burned, but love I have not, I am profited nothing.
verses.note: GREEK: "Psōmisō" (ψωμίσω, G5595) means "to feed by putting morsels into the mouth," implying hands-on, personal generosity. "Kauthēsomai" (καυθήσομαι) — some manuscripts read "kauchēsōmai" (that I may boast); the textual variant is significant. The reading "burned" suggests martyrdom. THEOLOGICAL: Even the most radical acts of self-sacrifice — total dispossession and bodily martyrdom — are spiritually profitless without love as their animating principle.

verses.flow: Love is patient, love is kind; love does not envy; love does not boast of itself, it is not puffed up,
verses.note: GREEK: "Makrothymei" (μακροθυμεῖ, G3114) literally means "long-tempered," the opposite of wrath. "Chrēsteuetai" (χρηστεύεται, G5541) is a NT hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the entire New Testament, meaning "shows itself kind" or "acts with gracious goodness." "Perpereuetai" (περπερεύεται, G4068) is also a hapax, meaning "to boast, to play the braggart." TYPOLOGY: This description of love mirrors the character of God Himself (cf. Exod 34:6; Ps 103:8), and in context describes the love embodied by Christ (cf. Phil 2:3-8).

verses.flow: it does not behave dishonorably, it does not seek the things of itself, it is not provoked, it does not reckon the evil,
it does not rejoice over unrighteousness, but rejoices together with the truth;
all things it bears, all things it believes, all things it hopes, all things it endures.
Love never falls. But whether prophecies, they shall be rendered inactive; whether tongues, they shall cease; whether knowledge, it shall be rendered inactive.
verses.note: GREEK: "Piptei" (πίπτει, G4098) — some manuscripts read "ekpiptei" (falls away); the sense is that love never collapses or becomes invalid. "Katargēthēsontai" (καταργηθήσονται, G2673) means "to be rendered inoperative, abolished, brought to nothing." Note that tongues use a different verb: "pausontai" (παύσονται, G3973), "they shall cease of themselves" (middle voice), suggesting tongues will simply stop on their own. THEOLOGICAL: The permanence of love over against the temporary nature of revelatory gifts establishes the eschatological framework: gifts serve the present age, love belongs to the age to come.

verses.flow: For out of a part we know, and out of a part we prophesy;
but when the perfect comes, that which is out of a part shall be rendered inactive.
When I was an infant, I spoke as an infant, I thought as an infant, I reasoned as an infant; when I have become a man, I have rendered inactive the things of the infant.
For we see now through a mirror in an enigma, but then face to face; now I know out of a part, but then I shall fully know even as also I was fully known.
verses.note: GREEK: "Esoptrou" (ἐσόπτρου, G2072) refers to a bronze mirror, for which Corinth was famous in manufacturing. "Ainigmati" (αἰνίγματι, G135) means "riddle, dark saying" — an allusion to Num 12:8 (LXX), where God speaks to Moses "mouth to mouth, clearly, and not in riddles (ainigmatōn)." "Epiginōskō" (ἐπιγνώσκω, G1921) is intensified over "ginōskō" — "to know fully, to recognize completely." TYPOLOGY: The "face to face" (prosōpon pros prosōpon) echoes Moses before God (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and anticipates the beatific vision when believers shall see the Lord as He is (1 John 3:2; Rev 22:4). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Present knowledge of divine mysteries is partial; full participatory knowledge awaits the eschatological unveiling when believers enter the divine presence.

verses.flow: But now abides faith, hope, love — these three; and the greatest of these is love.
verses.note: GREEK: "Nuni" (νυνί, G3570) is emphatic — "but now, as things stand." "Menei" (μένει, G3306) means "abides, remains, endures" — the same verb used of Christ's eternal priesthood (Heb 7:24). "Meizōn" (μείζων, G3187) is the comparative of "megas" functioning as a superlative: "greatest." THEOLOGICAL: Faith, hope, and love form a triad appearing repeatedly in Paul (cf. 1 Thess 1:3; Col 1:4-5; Rom 5:1-5). Love is greatest because it alone persists into the eschaton: faith gives way to sight, hope to fulfillment, but love endures as the very nature of God Himself (1 John 4:8).

verses.flow: Pursue love, and be zealous for the spiritual gifts, but rather that you may prophesy.
verses.note: GREEK: "Pursue" (διώκετε, diōkete) is a strong verb meaning to chase or press after, the same word used for persecution in reverse — here applied to love (agapē). "Be zealous" (ζηλοῦτε, zēloute) connotes passionate desire. Paul subordinates charismatic gifts to love, directly linking this exhortation to the great love discourse of chapter 13. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the hinge between the love chapter (ch. 13) and the practical regulation of gifts (ch. 14).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one speaking in a tongue does not speak to men but to God; for no one hears, but in the Spirit he speaks mysteries.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mysteries" (μυστήρια, mystēria, Strong's G3466) in Pauline usage denotes divine truths hidden from natural understanding but revealed by the Spirit. The term carries Second Temple apocalyptic overtones (cf. Dan 2:28 LXX). THEOLOGICAL: The one speaking in a tongue communicates Godward, not manward — the speech is genuine but unintelligible to human hearers without interpretation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one prophesying speaks to men edification and exhortation and consolation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one speaking in a tongue edifies himself, but the one prophesying edifies the assembly.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now I desire all of you to speak in tongues, but rather that you may prophesy; and greater is the one prophesying than the one speaking in tongues, unless he interprets, that the assembly may receive edification.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul does not disparage tongues but establishes a clear hierarchy for congregational use: prophecy is "greater" (μείζων, meizōn) because it edifies the whole assembly. The exception clause ("unless he interprets," ἐκτὸς εἰ μὴ διερμηνεύῃ) shows that interpreted tongues function equivalently to prophecy. GREEK: "Edification" (οἰκοδομή, oikodomē) is Paul's controlling metaphor — literally "house-building" — for the upbuilding of the body of Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now then, brothers, if I come to you speaking in tongues, what shall I profit you, unless I speak to you either in revelation, or in knowledge, or in prophecy, or in teaching?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: Likewise, the lifeless things giving a sound, whether flute or harp, if they give no distinction to the tones, how shall what is being played on the flute or what is being played on the harp be known?
For indeed if a trumpet gives an unclear sound, who shall prepare himself for battle?

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: So also you, through the tongue, unless you give a clear word, how shall what is being spoken be known? For you shall be speaking into the air.
There are, it may be, so many kinds of sounds in the world, and none of them is without meaning.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: If then I do not know the meaning of the sound, I shall be to the one speaking a foreigner, and the one speaking a foreigner to me.
So also you, since you are zealous for spirits, seek that you may abound unto the edification of the assembly.
verses.note: GREEK: "Zealous for spirits" (ζηλωταὶ πνευμάτων, zēlōtai pneumatōn) is a striking phrase — literally "zealots of spirits," meaning eager for spiritual manifestations. Paul redirects their zeal toward the communal goal: edification of the assembly (ekklēsia). THEOLOGICAL: The criterion for evaluating spiritual gifts is never personal experience but corporate benefit.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore let the one speaking in a tongue pray that he may interpret.
For if I pray in a tongue, my spirit prays, but my mind is unfruitful.
What then is it? I shall pray with the spirit, and I shall pray also with the mind; I shall sing psalms with the spirit, and I shall sing psalms also with the mind.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For if you bless in the spirit, how shall the one filling the place of the uninstructed say the "Amen" at your thanksgiving, since he does not know what you are saying?
verses.note: GREEK: "The one filling the place of the uninstructed" (ὁ ἀναπληρῶν τὸν τόπον τοῦ ἰδιώτου) — the term ἰδιώτης (idiōtēs, Strong's G2399) refers not to an "idiot" but to a layperson or one without specialized knowledge, possibly an inquirer or catechumen present in the assembly. HISTORICAL: The congregational "Amen" (cf. Deut 27:15-26; Neh 8:6) was a well-established Jewish liturgical practice adopted by the early church.

verses.flow: For you indeed give thanks well, but the other is not edified.
I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you;
but in the assembly I desire to speak five words with my mind, that I might also instruct others, rather than ten thousand words in a tongue.
Brothers, do not become children in understanding, but in evil be infants, and in understanding become mature.
In the law it has been written: "By men of other tongues and by lips of others I shall speak to this people, and not even so shall they hear me, says the Lord."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 28:11-12 (LXX) where God warned Israel that He would speak to them through foreign tongues — the Assyrian invaders — as a sign of judgment upon their unbelief. Paul applies this typologically: tongues function as a sign directed at unbelievers, echoing the original Isaianic context of covenant judgment. GREEK: "Men of other tongues" (ἑτερογλώσσοις, heteroglōssois) is a NT hapax legomenon, found only here.

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: So then tongues are for a sign, not to those who believe, but to the unbelieving; but prophecy is not for the unbelieving, but for those who believe.
If therefore the whole assembly comes together in one place, and all speak in tongues, and there enter uninstructed ones or unbelievers, will they not say that you are mad?
But if all prophesy, and some unbeliever or uninstructed one enters, he is convicted by all, he is examined by all;

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: the hidden things of his heart become manifest, and so, falling upon his face, he will worship God, declaring that God is truly among you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophetic unveiling of the heart's secrets produces worship — the unbeliever falls on his face (πεσὼν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον) and declares "God is truly among you." This echoes Isaiah 45:14 and Zechariah 8:23, where Gentiles recognize the God of Israel among His people. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The prophetic function here mirrors the heavenly court scene where hidden things are made manifest before the divine judge (cf. Dan 2:22; Heb 4:12-13).

verses.flow: What then is it, brothers? When you come together, each one has a psalm, has a teaching, has a revelation, has a tongue, has an interpretation. Let all things be done unto edification.
If anyone speaks in a tongue, let it be by two or at the most three, and each in turn, and let one interpret.
But if there is no interpreter, let him keep silent in the assembly, and let him speak to himself and to God.
And let two or three prophets speak, and let the others discern.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if something is revealed to another who is seated, let the first keep silent.
For you are all able to prophesy one by one, that all may learn and all may be exhorted.
And the spirits of prophets are subject to prophets;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The spirits of prophets are subject to prophets" establishes that genuine prophetic inspiration does not override human self-control. This distinguishes Christian prophecy from ecstatic mania in Greco-Roman religion where the medium lost volition. HISTORICAL: Pagan oracles at Delphi and other shrines involved loss of consciousness; Paul insists the Holy Spirit works through, not against, the rational will of the prophet.

verses.flow: for God is not a God of disorder but of peace, as in all the assemblies of the saints.
verses.note: GREEK: "Disorder" (ἀκαταστασία, akatastasia, Strong's G181) means confusion, instability, or tumult — the opposite of the divine character. THEOLOGICAL: God's nature as a God of peace (εἰρήνη, eirēnē) serves as the theological ground for liturgical order. This is not merely pragmatic counsel but reflects the very being of God. The phrase "as in all the assemblies of the saints" indicates Paul appeals to universal apostolic practice, not local custom.

verses.flow: Let the women keep silent in the assemblies, for it is not permitted to them to speak, but let them be in submission, as also the law says.
And if they desire to learn something, let them ask their own husbands at home; for it is shameful for a woman to speak in the assembly.
Or did the word of God go forth from you? Or did it reach you only?
If anyone considers himself to be a prophet or spiritual, let him recognize that the things I write to you are a commandment of the Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul makes a remarkable apostolic claim — what he writes is not merely pastoral advice but "a commandment of the Lord" (κυρίου ἐστίν, kyriou estin). This asserts the dominical authority behind apostolic instruction and the inscripturated nature of Christ's ongoing rule over His assemblies. GREEK: "Recognize" (ἐπιγινωσκέτω, epiginōsketō) implies deep, full acknowledgment — not casual awareness but authoritative discernment befitting one who claims to be spiritual.

verses.flow: But if anyone is ignorant, he is ignored.
So then, my brothers, be zealous for prophesying, and do not forbid speaking in tongues.
But let all things be done decently and according to order.
verses.note: GREEK: "Decently" (εὐσχημόνως, euschēmonōs) means with propriety and good form; "according to order" (κατὰ τάξιν, kata taxin) denotes arranged sequence. STRUCTURE: This verse functions as the concluding summary of the entire chapter's argument — all the regulations about tongues, prophecy, and assembly conduct are distilled into this single principle. The word τάξις (taxis) is also used of the priestly order of Melchizedek (Heb 5:6), suggesting that congregational worship has a sacred, ordered dignity.

verses.flow: Now I make known to you, brothers, the gospel which I proclaimed to you, which also you received, in which also you stand,
verses.note: GREEK: "Gnōrizō" (γνωρίζω, Strong's G1107) means "to make known, to certify" — Paul is not introducing new material but formally re-establishing what was already received. "Euangelion" (εὐαγγέλιον, G2098) carries both the sense of a royal proclamation and the good news of salvation. STRUCTURE: Verses 1-11 form a creedal prologue establishing the historical foundation of the resurrection before the theological argument of vv. 12-58.

verses.flow: through which also you are being saved, if you hold fast the word which I proclaimed to you — unless you believed in vain.
For I delivered to you among the first things that which I also received: that Christ died on behalf of our sins according to the Scriptures,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Among the first things" (ἐν πρώτοις) indicates this is the core apostolic tradition, pre-Pauline in origin, likely dating to within years of the crucifixion. "According to the Scriptures" (κατὰ τὰς γραφάς) points to passages such as Isaiah 53:5-12 and Psalm 22. TYPOLOGY: Christ's death "on behalf of" (ὑπέρ, G5228) our sins fulfills the Levitical sin-offering pattern, where the sacrifice bears the penalty in place of the offerer (Lev 16:15-22; Isa 53:5-6).

verses.flow: and that he was buried, and that he has been raised on the third day according to the Scriptures,
verses.note: GREEK: "Egēgertai" (ἐγήγερται) is a perfect passive indicative — the resurrection happened in the past with ongoing present results. Christ was raised and remains in a risen state. The passive voice implies God the Father as agent. TYPOLOGY: "The third day according to the Scriptures" echoes Hosea 6:2, Jonah 1:17 (cf. Matt 12:40), and the binding of Isaac on the third day (Gen 22:4).

verses.flow: and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve.
Then he appeared to more than five hundred brothers at once, of whom the greater number remain until now, but some have fallen asleep.
Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles.
And last of all, as to one untimely born, he appeared also to me.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ektrōma" (ἔκτρωμα, G1626) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "one untimely born, a miscarriage." Paul uses this jarring term of himself to emphasize the abnormal, violent, and gracious nature of his calling — he was not gestated through the normal apostolic process of following Jesus during his ministry but was seized by the risen Christ on the Damascus road (Acts 9:1-6).

verses.flow: For I am the least of the apostles, who am not worthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the assembly of God.
But by the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace toward me was not empty, but more abundantly than all of them I labored — yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.
Whether then it was I or they, so we proclaim, and so you believed.
Now if Christ is proclaimed that he has been raised from the dead, how do some among you say that there is no resurrection of the dead?
But if there is no resurrection of the dead, neither has Christ been raised.
And if Christ has not been raised, then empty is our proclamation, and empty also is your faith.
And we are found also to be false witnesses of God, because we testified against God that he raised Christ — whom he did not raise, if indeed the dead are not raised.
For if the dead are not raised, neither has Christ been raised.
And if Christ has not been raised, vain is your faith; you are still in your sins.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Then also those who have fallen asleep in Christ have perished.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: If in this life only we have hoped in Christ, we are of all men most pitiable.

words.m: verb perfect middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But now Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "But now" (Νυνὶ δέ) marks the dramatic pivot of the entire chapter — from hypothetical despair to triumphant reality. TYPOLOGY: "Aparchē" (ἀπαρχή, G536), "firstfruits," directly invokes the Feast of Firstfruits (Lev 23:10-11), where the first sheaf of the harvest was waved before the Lord as a pledge guaranteeing the full harvest to come. Christ's resurrection is the guarantee of the general resurrection. His rising on "the day after the Sabbath" during Passover week aligns precisely with this feast.

verses.flow: For since through a man came death, through a man also came the resurrection of the dead.
For just as in Adam all die, so also in Christ all shall be made alive.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul establishes a comprehensive Adam-Christ typology (cf. Rom 5:12-21). Adam as federal head brought death to all in his line; Christ as the last Adam and true federal head brings life to all in his line. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The scope of Christ's headship surpasses Adam's original dominion mandate (Gen 1:28), extending to cosmic restoration over all created orders, visible and invisible.

verses.flow: But each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then those who are Christ's at his coming,
then the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God even the Father, when he has abolished all rule and all authority and power.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The end" (τὸ τέλος) refers to the consummation of all things when Christ delivers the kingdom to God the Father. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "All rule and all authority and power" (πᾶσαν ἀρχὴν καὶ πᾶσαν ἐξουσίαν καὶ δύναμιν) encompasses both human and cosmic spiritual powers — the rebellious members of the divine council and all hostile supernatural hierarchies (cf. Eph 1:21, 6:12; Col 2:15). Christ's reign systematically dismantles every rival claim to authority in heaven and on earth.

verses.flow: For he must reign until he has placed all enemies under his feet.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Psalm 110:1, the most cited OT passage in the NT, which portrays the Davidic King-Priest seated at God's right hand while God places all enemies beneath his feet. This is the foundational messianic enthronement text. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The subjugation of enemies "under his feet" echoes the ancient Near Eastern motif of divine warrior victory and the Psalm 82 judgment against rebellious divine beings.

verses.flow: The last enemy to be abolished is death.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: For "he subjected all things under his feet." But when it says that all things have been subjected, it is clear that this is apart from the One who subjected all things to him.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He subjected all things under his feet" quotes Psalm 8:6 (LXX), originally describing Adam's dominion over creation. Paul applies this to Christ as the true and final Adam who fulfills the mandate that the first Adam forfeited. THEOLOGICAL: The careful exception — "apart from the One who subjected all things to him" — preserves the distinction between Father and Son while affirming the Son's universal sovereignty under the Father's authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when all things have been subjected to him, then the Son himself also shall be subjected to the One who subjected all things to him, so that God may be all in all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Son's voluntary subjection to the Father after all things are subjected to him is not a diminution of deity but the telos of the economic Trinity — the eternal purpose fulfilled, with God as "all in all" (πάντα ἐν πᾶσιν). This is the ultimate restoration of divine order over all creation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "God all in all" signifies the complete elimination of all rival claims to divine authority — every hostile power in the heavenly and earthly realms fully subdued under the one true God.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Otherwise, what shall they do who are being baptized on behalf of the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why then are they baptized on behalf of them?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Baptized on behalf of the dead" (βαπτιζόμενοι ὑπὲρ τῶν νεκρῶν) is one of the most debated phrases in the NT. Over forty interpretations have been proposed. The most contextually natural reading is that some Corinthians practiced vicarious baptism for deceased believers — Paul neither endorses nor condemns the practice but uses it as an ad hominem argument: even this practice presupposes the reality of resurrection. GREEK: "Hyper" (ὑπέρ, G5228) here likely means "on behalf of" or "for the sake of."

verses.flow: Why also do we endanger ourselves every hour?
I die daily — I affirm it by the boasting in you which I have in Christ Jesus our Lord.
If according to man I fought with wild beasts at Ephesus, what is the profit to me? If the dead are not raised, "Let us eat and let us drink, for tomorrow we die."
Do not be deceived: "Bad associations corrupt good character."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Bad associations corrupt good character" (φθείρουσιν ἤθη χρηστὰ ὁμιλίαι κακαί) is a quotation from Menander's comedy "Thais" (fragment 218), demonstrating Paul's familiarity with Greek literary culture. GREEK: "Ēthē chrēsta" (ἤθη χρηστά) means "good morals/character" and "homiliai kakai" (ὁμιλίαι κακαί) means "evil companionships/associations." Paul warns that denying the resurrection has moral consequences — theology shapes ethics.

verses.flow: Sober up righteously and do not sin, for some have ignorance of God — I say this to your shame.
But someone will say, "How are the dead raised? And with what kind of body do they come?"
Fool! What you sow is not made alive unless it dies.

words.m: verb future middle participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And what you sow — you do not sow the body that shall come to be, but a bare grain, perhaps of wheat or of some other kind.
But God gives it a body just as he willed, and to each of the seeds its own body.
Not all flesh is the same flesh, but one indeed is of men, and another flesh of beasts, and another flesh of birds, and another of fish.
And there are heavenly bodies and earthly bodies; but the glory of the heavenly is one, and the glory of the earthly is another.
There is one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars; for star differs from star in glory.
So also is the resurrection of the dead. It is sown in corruption; it is raised in incorruption.
It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power.
It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body. If there is a natural body, there is also a spiritual body.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: So also it is written: "The first man Adam became a living soul." The last Adam became a life-giving spirit.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Genesis 2:7 (LXX) — "the first man Adam became a living soul (ψυχὴν ζῶσαν)" — and then adds his own christological declaration: "the last Adam became a life-giving spirit (πνεῦμα ζῳοποιοῦν)." The contrast is stark: the first Adam received life; the last Adam gives life. THEOLOGICAL: "Last Adam" (ὁ ἔσχατος Ἀδάμ) is not merely "another" Adam but the final, eschatological Adam who definitively accomplishes what the first Adam was meant to embody — the image-bearing ruler who brings life rather than death.

verses.flow: But the spiritual is not first, but the natural; then the spiritual.
The first man is from the earth, earthy; the second man is from heaven.
As is the earthy one, such also are those who are earthy; and as is the heavenly one, such also are those who are heavenly.
And just as we have borne the image of the earthy, we shall bear also the image of the heavenly.
verses.note: GREEK: The textual variant here is significant — some manuscripts read "phorésōmen" (φορέσωμεν, subjunctive: "let us bear") while others read "phorésomen" (φορέσομεν, future indicative: "we shall bear"). The future indicative is preferred as it fits Paul's argument: the bearing of the heavenly image is a certain eschatological promise, not merely an exhortation. TYPOLOGY: "Image" (εἰκών, G1504) recalls Genesis 1:26-27 — believers will bear the fully restored and glorified image of the heavenly man, Christ (cf. Rom 8:29; 2 Cor 3:18).

verses.flow: Now this I say, brothers, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does corruption inherit incorruption.
Behold, I tell you a mystery: we shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed,
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystērion" (μυστήριον, G3466) does not mean "mystery" in the modern sense of something unknowable, but a divine secret now being revealed. THEOLOGICAL: Paul reveals new eschatological information: not all believers will die ("sleep"), but all will be transformed. This distinguishes between resurrection of the dead and instantaneous transformation of the living at Christ's return (cf. 1 Thess 4:15-17).

verses.flow: in an instant, in a twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed.
verses.note: GREEK: "Atomō" (ἀτόμῳ, G823) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning literally "un-cuttable" — the smallest indivisible unit of time, an instant. "Rhipē ophthalmou" (ῥιπῇ ὀφθαλμοῦ) means "a twinkling/flashing of an eye." Both terms emphasize the instantaneous nature of the transformation. THEOLOGICAL: "The last trumpet" (ἐσχάτῃ σάλπιγγι) connects to the theophanic trumpet tradition of Sinai (Exod 19:16), the trumpet of divine assembly and warfare (Num 10:1-10), and the eschatological trumpet of 1 Thessalonians 4:16.

verses.flow: For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when this corruptible has put on incorruption, and this mortal has put on immortality, then shall come to pass the word that is written: "Death is swallowed up in victory."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Death is swallowed up in victory" quotes Isaiah 25:8 (LXX), from the Isaianic apocalypse describing the eschatological banquet on Mount Zion when God destroys the shroud of death over all peoples. Paul declares that this ancient prophetic hope finds its fulfillment in the bodily resurrection of believers through Christ. THEOLOGICAL: The verb "katepothē" (κατεπόθη, "was swallowed up") is a divine passive — God is the one who consumes death itself, the ultimate enemy.

verses.flow: "Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul adapts Hosea 13:14 (LXX), transforming what in its original context was a threat of judgment into a triumphant taunt over defeated death. "Kentron" (κέντρον, G2759) means "sting" or "goad" — the venomous barb by which death inflicts its wound. Through Christ's resurrection, death's weapon has been rendered powerless. THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical questions presuppose the answer: nowhere. Death's victory and sting have been abolished.

verses.flow: Now the sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The victory (νῖκος, G3534) over death is not achieved by human effort but is a gift — "the One who gives us" (τῷ διδόντι ἡμῖν), present participle indicating ongoing, continuous giving. This victory comes "through our Lord Jesus Christ" (διὰ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ) — the full christological title emphasizing his sovereignty as Lord, his identity as Jesus of Nazareth, and his office as the Anointed King.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not empty in the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: "Hedraioi" (ἑδραῖοι, G1476) means "seated, settled, firm" and "ametakinētoi" (ἀμετακίνητοι, G277) means "unmovable" — together they paint a picture of unshakeable stability. THEOLOGICAL: The practical conclusion of the chapter: because the resurrection is certain, present labor in the Lord is never "empty" (κενός, G2756) — the same word used in vv. 10, 14 to describe what faith and proclamation would be without the resurrection. The resurrection guarantees that all service rendered to Christ has eternal significance and weight.

verses.flow: Now concerning the collection for the holy ones, just as I directed the assemblies of Galatia, so also you do.
verses.note: GREEK: λογεία (logeia, Strong's G3048) is a rare term for "collection" appearing only here and v.2 in the NT. It was used in Egyptian papyri for religious tax collections. HISTORICAL: Paul organized a collection from Gentile assemblies for the impoverished saints in Jerusalem, a project central to his ministry (Rom 15:25-27; 2 Cor 8-9), symbolizing the unity of Jew and Gentile in one body.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: On the first day of the week, let each one of you set aside by himself, storing up whatever he may be prospered in, so that when I come there need not then be collections.
verses.note: GREEK: κατὰ μίαν σαββάτου (kata mian sabbatou) literally "on the first [day] of the week." This is early evidence that believers gathered on the first day, the day of the Lord's resurrection. θησαυρίζων (thēsaurizōn, Strong's G2343) means "treasuring up, storing away" — each believer proportionally sets aside as God has prospered them.

verses.flow: And when I arrive, whomever you approve, these I will send with letters to carry your gift to Jerusalem.
verses.note: GREEK: χάριν (charin) here means "gift" or "gracious work," not merely "grace" in the theological sense. Paul uses the language of grace even for monetary giving, showing that generosity is itself an expression of divine grace (2 Cor 8:1-4). HISTORICAL: Letters of commendation were standard practice to authenticate envoys in the ancient world.

verses.flow: And if it be worthy for me also to go, they shall go with me.
But I shall come to you when I pass through Macedonia, for I am passing through Macedonia.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And with you perhaps I shall remain, or even winter, so that you may send me on my way wherever I may go.
For I do not wish to see you now in passing, for I hope to remain some time with you, if the Lord permits.
But I shall remain in Ephesus until Pentecost,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ephesus was the capital of the Roman province of Asia and a major center of commerce and pagan worship, especially of Artemis. Paul's extended stay there indicates its strategic importance for the gospel mission. Pentecost (πεντηκοστή, pentēkostē) refers to the Jewish feast fifty days after Passover, showing Paul still reckoned time by the Jewish liturgical calendar.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: for a door has opened to me, great and effectual, and there are many adversaries.
verses.note: GREEK: θύρα μεγάλη καὶ ἐνεργής (thyra megalē kai energēs) — "a great and effectual door." ἐνεργής (energēs, Strong's G1756) means "active, productive, at work." THEOLOGICAL: Paul pairs great opportunity with great opposition — a pattern throughout apostolic ministry. The open door is a divine provision (cf. Col 4:3; Rev 3:8), yet adversaries (ἀντικείμενοι) are many, reflecting spiritual warfare accompanying gospel advance.

verses.flow: Now if Timothy comes, see that he be without fear among you, for the work of the Lord he works, as I also do.
Let no one therefore despise him. But send him on his way in peace, so that he may come to me, for I await him with the brothers.
Now concerning Apollos the brother, I urged him greatly that he should come to you with the brothers, and it was not at all his will to come now; but he will come when he has opportunity.
Be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act like men, be strengthened.
verses.note: GREEK: Five imperatives in rapid succession form a military-style exhortation. γρηγορεῖτε (grēgoreite, "be watchful"), στήκετε (stēkete, "stand firm"), ἀνδρίζεσθε (andrizesthe, "act like men/be courageous"), κραταιοῦσθε (krataiousthe, "be strengthened/made mighty"). TYPOLOGY: The language echoes the LXX of Josh 1:6-9 and Ps 31:24 (LXX 30:25), the exhortation given to Joshua and to Israel — courage in the face of spiritual battle as the people of God advance.

verses.flow: Let all your things be done in love.
Now I urge you, brothers — you know the household of Stephanas, that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and they devoted themselves to the ministry of the holy ones —
verses.note: GREEK: ἀπαρχή (aparchē, Strong's G536) means "firstfruits," a sacrificial and agricultural term (cf. Rom 16:5). TYPOLOGY: The firstfruits offering (Lev 23:10) consecrated the whole harvest to God. Stephanas's household, as the first converts of Achaia, consecrated the entire region's harvest of souls. Their self-appointed (ἔταξαν ἑαυτούς) service to the holy ones models voluntary devotion.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: that you also be subject to such ones, and to everyone who works together and labors.
And I rejoice at the coming of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus, because what was lacking on your part, these men filled up.
For they refreshed my spirit and yours. Therefore acknowledge such men as these.
The assemblies of Asia greet you. Aquila and Prisca greet you much in the Lord, together with the assembly at their house.
All the brothers greet you. Greet one another with a holy kiss.
The greeting by my own hand — Paul's.
If anyone does not love the Lord, let him be accursed. Marana tha!
verses.note: GREEK: φιλεῖ (philei, Strong's G5368) here denotes personal affection and devotion toward the Lord — not merely intellectual assent. ἀνάθεμα (anathema, Strong's G331) means "accursed, devoted to destruction," from the LXX concept of חֵרֶם (cherem). THEOLOGICAL: Μαράνα θά (Marana tha) is Aramaic meaning "Our Lord, come!" — a primitive liturgical prayer preserved untranslated, attesting to the earliest Aramaic-speaking church's worship of Jesus as Lord and their eager expectation of His return (cf. Rev 22:20). Its preservation in a Greek letter to Gentiles shows the universal scope of this confession.

verses.flow: The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.
My love be with all of you in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul closes with a declaration of love (ἀγάπη, agapē) encompassing all believers "in Christ Jesus" — the sphere of union that defines the community. This warm personal closing counterbalances the severe corrections throughout the letter, showing that apostolic discipline is always rooted in covenantal love.
